topics.title: Definition of the Laplace transform; Laplace transforms of some basic forcing functions - step, exponential, ramp, sinusoidal, cosine, pulse, impulse and translated functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT601 - PROCESS DYNAMICS CONTROL & INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definition of the Laplace transform; Laplace transforms of some basic forcing functions - step, exponential, ramp, sinusoidal, cosine, pulse, impulse and translated functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the Laplace Transform

The **Laplace Transform** is a powerful integral transform that converts a time-domain function $f(t)$ into a complex frequency-domain function $F(s)$. Mathematically, the **unilateral (one-sided) Laplace transform** is defined as:

$$F(s) \;=\; \mathcal{L}\{f(t)\} \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} f(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt$$

where:
- $f(t)$ is a time-domain function defined for $t \ge 0$
- $s = \sigma + j\omega$ is a complex variable (the **Laplace operator**)
- $\sigma$ is the real part (ensures convergence) and $\omega$ is the imaginary part (frequency)
- $F(s)$ is the transformed function in the s-domain
- The transformation is denoted symbolically as $\mathcal{L}\{\,\cdot\,\}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The unilateral Laplace transform (starting at $t = 0^-$ or $0^+$) is the standard tool in process control. It automatically handles initial conditions of differential equations, which is why it is preferred over Fourier transforms for solving ODEs in control engineering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Existence Condition:** The Laplace transform exists if the integral $\int_{0}^{\infty} \vert f(t) e^{-st} \vert\, dt$ is finite. For functions of exponential order (i.e., $\vert f(t)\vert \le M e^{at}$ for some constants $M$ and $a$), the transform converges for $s > a$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Time-Machine" of Engineering

Imagine you are listening to a musical recording on a tape. The **time-domain** is the song as you hear it — second by second, note by note. The **Laplace transform** is like a sophisticated audio analyzer that breaks the song into its underlying **frequency components** (bass, treble, harmonics) and presents them in a structured spectrum.

**In process control**, the Laplace transform is the "Rosetta Stone" that translates:
- **Differential equations** (hard to solve) ➜ **Algebraic equations** (easy to solve)
- **Convolution integrals** (complex operations) ➜ **Simple multiplications**
- **Time-response analysis** (slow simulation) ➜ **Transfer function analysis** (rapid design)

Think of $f(t)$ as the **input signal** to a chemical reactor, and $F(s)$ as its **"fingerprint"** in the frequency domain — capturing the essence of how the input behaves across all frequencies simultaneously.

## 1.3 The Inverse Laplace Transform

To recover $f(t)$ from $F(s)$, we use the **Bromwich integral**:

$$f(t) \;=\; \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{F(s)\} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi j} \int_{\gamma - j\infty}^{\gamma + j\infty} F(s)\, e^{st}\, ds$$

In practice, partial-fraction expansion and standard transform tables are used instead of evaluating this complex integral.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Engineers Love Laplace:** A 3rd-order ODE becomes a 3rd-degree polynomial in $s$. Roots of the characteristic equation immediately tell you if the system is **stable**, **marginally stable**, or **unstable** — without solving a single differential equation!

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why the Laplace Transform is Indispensable in Process Control

The Laplace transform is the **mathematical backbone** of classical control theory. Its key advantages are:

1. **Linearization of Calculus:** Differentiation and integration in time become multiplication and division by $s$ in the s-domain.
2. **Initial Conditions:** Automatically incorporated; no need for arbitrary constants.
3. **System Characterization:** A system's input-output behavior is captured by a single **transfer function** $G(s) = Y(s)/X(s)$.
4. **Stability Analysis:** Pole locations (roots of denominator) directly determine stability.
5. **Convolution Simplification:** Convolution in time becomes multiplication in s-domain.

## 2.2 The Strategic Process of Deriving Transforms

For each forcing function, the derivation follows a **standard 3-step protocol**:

- **Step 1:** Identify the function $f(t)$ for $t \ge 0$ (causal region).
- **Step 2:** Substitute into the defining integral $\int_{0}^{\infty} f(t) e^{-st} dt$.
- **Step 3:** Evaluate using standard integration rules (or known results), yielding $F(s)$ valid for $\text{Re}(s) > a$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Basic Forcing Functions

The following table is the **single most important reference** for Module 1 problems. **Memorize these cold.**

| # | Time-Domain Function $f(t)$ | Name | Laplace Transform $F(s) = \mathcal{L}\{f(t)\}$ | Region of Convergence |
|---|----------------------------|--------------------|------------------------------------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | $1$ (unit step) | Unit Step $u(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{s}$ | $s > 0$ |
| 2 | $t$ | Unit Ramp | $\dfrac{1}{s^{2}}$ | $s > 0$ |
| 3 | $t^{n}$ ($n = 1,2,3,\ldots$) | Polynomial / $n$th-order ramp | $\dfrac{n!}{s^{n+1}}$ | $s > 0$ |
| 4 | $e^{-at}$ | Exponential Decay | $\dfrac{1}{s + a}$ | $s > -a$ |
| 5 | $1 - e^{-t/\tau}$ | First-order response | $\dfrac{1}{s(\tau s + 1)}$ | $s > 0$ |
| 6 | $\sin(\omega t)$ | Sine wave | $\dfrac{\omega}{s^{2} + \omega^{2}}$ | $s > 0$ |
| 7 | $\cos(\omega t)$ | Cosine wave | $\dfrac{s}{s^{2} + \omega^{2}}$ | $s > 0$ |
| 8 | $e^{-at}\sin(\omega t)$ | Damped sine | $\dfrac{\omega}{(s + a)^{2} + \omega^{2}}$ | $s > -a$ |
| 9 | $e^{-at}\cos(\omega t)$ | Damped cosine | $\dfrac{s + a}{(s + a)^{2} + \omega^{2}}$ | $s > -a$ |
| 10 | $\delta(t)$ | Unit Impulse | $1$ | All $s$ |
| 11 | Pulse $p_{h}(t) = \frac{1}{h}[u(t) - u(t-h)]$ | Rectangular pulse | $\dfrac{1 - e^{-hs}}{hs}$ | All $s$ |
| 12 | $f(t - a) \cdot u(t - a)$ | Translated / Time-shifted | $e^{-as} F(s)$ | Depends on $f$ |
| 13 | $t \cdot e^{-at}$ | $t$-weighted exponential | $\dfrac{1}{(s + a)^{2}}$ | $s > -a$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Every entry in this table corresponds to a **real physical input** in process control:
> - Step $u(t)$ → sudden valve opening, setpoint change, load disturbance
> - Ramp → gradual setpoint ramp, slowly drifting input
> - Exponential → first-order system response, RC circuit charging
> - Sine/Cosine → sinusoidal pressure oscillation, AC disturbances
> - Impulse → instantaneous load shock, hammer impact
> - Pulse → batch addition, finite-duration input
> - Translated → inputs that begin at $t = a$ instead of $t = 0$

## 2.4 Critical Properties Used in Derivations

| Property | Time Domain | s-Domain |
|----------|-------------|----------|
| Linearity | $a f(t) + b g(t)$ | $a F(s) + b G(s)$ |
| Time shift | $f(t - a) u(t - a)$ | $e^{-as} F(s)$ |
| s-shift (frequency shift) | $e^{-at} f(t)$ | $F(s + a)$ |
| Differentiation | $\dfrac{df(t)}{dt}$ | $s F(s) - f(0)$ |
| Integration | $\int_{0}^{t} f(\tau) d\tau$ | $\dfrac{F(s)}{s}$ |
| Final value | $\lim_{t \to \infty} f(t)$ | $\lim_{s \to 0} s F(s)$ |
| Initial value | $\lim_{t \to 0^{+}} f(t)$ | $\lim_{s \to \infty} s F(s)$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Value Theorem Caveat:** The final value theorem $\lim_{s \to 0} sF(s)$ is **valid only if all poles of $sF(s)$ lie in the left-half s-plane (i.e., the system is stable).** Using it on an unstable system gives incorrect (misleading) results — a common KTU exam trap.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU board exams, you may be asked to *derive* any of the basic transforms. Master these derivations — they are worth **3 to 7 marks** depending on depth.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Unit Step Function $f(t) = u(t) = 1$

The unit step function is defined as:
$$u(t) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1, & t \ge 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

Applying the Laplace definition:

$$\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} (1) \cdot e^{-st}\, dt$$

$$=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-st}\, dt$$

$$=\; \left[ \frac{e^{-st}}{-s} \right]_{0}^{\infty}$$

$$=\; \left( \lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{e^{-st}}{-s} \right) - \left( \frac{e^{0}}{-s} \right)$$

$$=\; 0 - \left( -\frac{1}{s} \right)$$

$$\boxed{\;\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} \;=\; \frac{1}{s}\;}, \quad s > 0$$

> **Note:** The limit $\lim_{t \to \infty} e^{-st} = 0$ only when $\text{Re}(s) > 0$, which defines the region of convergence.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Unit Ramp Function $f(t) = t$

For $t \ge 0$:

$$\mathcal{L}\{t\} \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot e^{-st}\, dt$$

This requires **integration by parts** with $u = t$ and $dv = e^{-st} dt$:

$$du \;=\; dt, \quad v \;=\; -\frac{e^{-st}}{s}$$

Applying $\int u\, dv = uv - \int v\, du$:

$$\mathcal{L}\{t\} \;=\; \left[ t \cdot \left( -\frac{e^{-st}}{s} \right) \right]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} \left( -\frac{e^{-st}}{s} \right) dt$$

$$=\; \left[ -\frac{t e^{-st}}{s} \right]_{0}^{\infty} + \frac{1}{s} \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-st}\, dt$$

For the first term, applying **L'Hôpital's rule** at $t \to \infty$ (since $\infty \cdot 0$ form):

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{t}{e^{st}} \;=\; \lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{1}{s e^{st}} \;=\; 0$$

So the first term equals $0 - 0 = 0$. The second term reduces to the step-function integral:

$$=\; 0 + \frac{1}{s} \cdot \left( \frac{1}{s} \right)$$

$$\boxed{\;\mathcal{L}\{t\} \;=\; \frac{1}{s^{2}}\;}, \quad s > 0$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Exponential Function $f(t) = e^{-at}$ (for $a$ constant)

$$\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at}\} \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-at} \cdot e^{-st}\, dt \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(s+a)t}\, dt$$

$$=\; \left[ \frac{e^{-(s+a)t}}{-(s+a)} \right]_{0}^{\infty} \;=\; \frac{0 - 1}{-(s+a)} \;=\; \frac{1}{s + a}$$

$$\boxed{\;\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at}\} \;=\; \frac{1}{s + a}\;}, \quad s > -a$$

> **Engineering Note:** This is the most common transform in process control — the response of a first-order system $\tau \frac{dy}{dt} + y = K u(t)$ is a pure exponential when $u(t) = 1$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Sine Function $f(t) = \sin(\omega t)$

Using **Euler's identity**:
$$\sin(\omega t) \;=\; \frac{e^{j\omega t} - e^{-j\omega t}}{2j}$$

Substituting:

$$\mathcal{L}\{\sin(\omega t)\} \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{e^{j\omega t} - e^{-j\omega t}}{2j} \cdot e^{-st}\, dt$$

$$=\; \frac{1}{2j} \int_{0}^{\infty} \left[ e^{-(s - j\omega)t} - e^{-(s + j\omega)t} \right] dt$$

$$=\; \frac{1}{2j} \left[ \frac{1}{s - j\omega} - \frac{1}{s + j\omega} \right]$$

Combining over a common denominator:

$$=\; \frac{1}{2j} \cdot \frac{(s + j\omega) - (s - j\omega)}{(s - j\omega)(s + j\omega)}$$

$$=\; \frac{1}{2j} \cdot \frac{2j\omega}{s^{2} + \omega^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{\;\mathcal{L}\{\sin(\omega t)\} \;=\; \frac{\omega}{s^{2} + \omega^{2}}\;}, \quad s > 0$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Cosine Function $f(t) = \cos(\omega t)$

Using **Euler's identity**:
$$\cos(\omega t) \;=\; \frac{e^{j\omega t} + e^{-j\omega t}}{2}$$

Substituting into the Laplace integral:

$$\mathcal{L}\{\cos(\omega t)\} \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{e^{j\omega t} + e^{-j\omega t}}{2} \cdot e^{-st}\, dt$$

$$=\; \frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{\infty} \left[ e^{-(s - j\omega)t} + e^{-(s + j\omega)t} \right] dt$$

$$=\; \frac{1}{2} \left[ \frac{1}{s - j\omega} + \frac{1}{s + j\omega} \right]$$

Common denominator:

$$=\; \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{(s + j\omega) + (s - j\omega)}{s^{2} + \omega^{2}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2s}{s^{2} + \omega^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{\;\mathcal{L}\{\cos(\omega t)\} \;=\; \frac{s}{s^{2} + \omega^{2}}\;}, \quad s > 0$$

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Unit Impulse Function $f(t) = \delta(t)$

The **Dirac delta** is a generalized function satisfying:
- $\delta(t) = 0$ for $t \ne 0$
- $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t) dt = 1$

Using the **sifting property** $\int_{0}^{\infty} \delta(t) g(t) dt = g(0)$:

$$\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\} \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} \delta(t) \cdot e^{-st}\, dt \;=\; e^{-s \cdot 0} \;=\; e^{0}$$

$$\boxed{\;\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\} \;=\; 1\;}$$

> **Engineering Note:** A unit impulse delivers an *infinite magnitude over an infinitesimal duration* such that its integral is unity. It models instantaneous shocks, lightning strikes, or an idealised sudden load.

---

## 3.7 Derivation 7: Rectangular Pulse Function

A rectangular pulse of unit area, width $h$, and height $1/h$:

$$p_{h}(t) \;=\; \frac{1}{h}\left[ u(t) - u(t - h) \right]$$

By **linearity** and the **time-shift property**:

$$\mathcal{L}\{p_{h}(t)\} \;=\; \frac{1}{h} \left[ \mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} - \mathcal{L}\{u(t - h)\} \right]$$

$$=\; \frac{1}{h} \left[ \frac{1}{s} - \frac{e^{-hs}}{s} \right] \;=\; \frac{1 - e^{-hs}}{hs}$$

$$\boxed{\;\mathcal{L}\{p_{h}(t)\} \;=\; \frac{1 - e^{-hs}}{hs}\;}$$

> **Verification (Limit Check):** As $h \to 0$, applying L'Hôpital:
> $$\lim_{h \to 0} \frac{1 - e^{-hs}}{hs} \;=\; \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{s e^{-hs}}{s} \;=\; 1 = \mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\}$$
> ✓ A narrow pulse converges to an impulse, consistent with physical intuition.

---

## 3.8 Derivation 8: Translated Function (Second Shifting Theorem)

If $f(t)$ has transform $F(s)$ and $a > 0$, then for the **time-shifted** function:

$$g(t) \;=\; f(t - a) \cdot u(t - a) \;=\; \begin{cases} f(t - a), & t \ge a \\ 0, & t < a \end{cases}$$

Substituting into the Laplace integral (with substitution $\tau = t - a$, so $d\tau = dt$):

$$\mathcal{L}\{g(t)\} \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} f(t - a) u(t - a)\, e^{-st}\, dt$$

Since $u(t-a) = 0$ for $t < a$, the lower limit becomes $a$:

$$=\; \int_{a}^{\infty} f(t - a)\, e^{-st}\, dt$$

Let $\tau = t - a \Rightarrow t = \tau + a$, and when $t = a$, $\tau = 0$; when $t \to \infty$, $\tau \to \infty$:

$$=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} f(\tau)\, e^{-s(\tau + a)}\, d\tau$$

$$=\; e^{-sa} \int_{0}^{\infty} f(\tau)\, e^{-s\tau}\, d\tau \;=\; e^{-sa} F(s)$$

$$\boxed{\;\mathcal{L}\{f(t - a) u(t - a)\} \;=\; e^{-as} F(s)\;}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** A delay of $a$ seconds in time corresponds to multiplication by $e^{-as}$ in the s-domain. This is fundamental in modeling **dead-time processes** (transportation delay, time lag in pipelines).

---

## 3.9 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference Summary

| Function | Transform | Key Identity Used |
|----------|-----------|-------------------|
| $u(t)$ | $1/s$ | Direct integration |
| $t$ | $1/s^2$ | Integration by parts |
| $e^{-at}$ | $1/(s+a)$ | Direct integration |
| $\sin\omega t$ | $\omega/(s^2+\omega^2)$ | Euler's identity |
| $\cos\omega t$ | $s/(s^2+\omega^2)$ | Euler's identity |
| $\delta(t)$ | $1$ | Sifting property |
| Pulse (width $h$) | $(1-e^{-hs})/hs$ | Linearity + time shift |
| $f(t-a)u(t-a)$ | $e^{-as}F(s)$ | Time-shift theorem |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: Laplace Transform Workflow

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the operational flow of applying the Laplace transform in a process-control problem:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Time-Domain Input f of t] --> B{Is f of t a known forcing function?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Look up standard transform table]
    B -- No --> D[Apply definition integral]
    D --> E[Evaluate using integration rules]
    C --> F[Output F of s in s-domain]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Solve algebraic equation in s]
    G --> H[Apply inverse Laplace]
    H --> I[Time-domain solution y of t]
    I --> J[Analyze transient and steady state]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    style F fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style I fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style J fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Start with the physical input $f(t)$ (a step, ramp, sine, etc.), pass it through the Laplace transform machinery to obtain $F(s)$, solve the resulting algebraic equation in the s-domain, then transform back to get the time-domain response $y(t)$ for stability and performance analysis.

## 4.2 Function-to-Transform Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Unit Step u of t] -- L --> A2[1 over s]
    A3[Unit Ramp t] -- L --> A4[1 over s squared]
    A5[Exponential e to power minus a t] -- L --> A6[1 over s plus a]
    A7[Sine omega t] -- L --> A8[omega over s squared plus omega squared]
    A9[Cosine omega t] -- L --> A10[s over s squared plus omega squared]
    A11[Impulse delta of t] -- L --> A12[1]
    A13[Pulse width h] -- L --> A14[1 minus e to power minus h s over h s]
    A15[Translated f of t minus a] -- L --> A16[e to power minus a s times F of s]

    style A2 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style A4 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style A6 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style A8 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style A10 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style A12 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style A14 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style A16 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
```

## 4.3 Typical Forcing Functions — Visual Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph FORCING_FUNCTIONS[Forcing Functions in Process Control]
        A1[Step u of t: sudden setpoint change]
        A2[Ramp t: gradual drift]
        A3[Exponential e to power minus a t: first order response]
        A4[Sine omega t: oscillating disturbance]
        A5[Impulse delta of t: sudden shock]
        A6[Pulse: batch input]
        A7[Translated f of t minus a u of t minus a: delayed input]
    end

    style FORCING_FUNCTIONS fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
```

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** Notice that **$1/s$** appears in *every* transform involving time-domain functions that are bounded as $t \to \infty$. This is because $1/s$ represents the integral $\int e^{-st} dt$ — the "dampener" that allows the transform to converge.

# 📘 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** 
**Define the Laplace transform. State the condition for its existence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** The Laplace transform of a time-domain function $f(t)$, defined for $t \ge 0$, is given by
> $$F(s) = \mathcal{L}\{f(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} f(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt$$
> where $s = \sigma + j\omega$ is a complex variable.

> **Existence Condition (1 mark):** The Laplace transform exists if $f(t)$ is **piecewise continuous** on $[0, \infty)$ and is of **exponential order**, i.e., there exist positive constants $M$ and $a$ such that $\vert f(t) \vert \le M e^{at}$ for all $t \ge 0$.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series-1]**
**Find the Laplace transform of $f(t) = 5\, e^{-3t} \sin(4t)$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Using the **s-shift property** $\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at} g(t)\} = G(s+a)$ with $a = 3$, and the standard result $\mathcal{L}\{\sin(4t)\} = \dfrac{4}{s^2 + 16}$:
> $$\mathcal{L}\{5\, e^{-3t} \sin(4t)\} \;=\; 5 \cdot \frac{4}{(s + 3)^2 + 16} \;=\; \frac{20}{s^2 + 6s + 25}$$
> 
> Expanding the denominator: $(s+3)^2 + 16 = s^2 + 6s + 9 + 16 = s^2 + 6s + 25$.
> $$\boxed{F(s) = \frac{20}{s^2 + 6s + 25}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. You must attempt **ONE full question** out of the two alternatives.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Define the Laplace transform of a function $f(t)$. Derive the Laplace transform of the unit step function and the unit ramp function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Laplace transforms of $e^{-at}$ and $\sin(\omega t)$ using the first principles (Laplace integral definition). **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]**

> **Definition (2 marks):**
> The Laplace transform of a causal function $f(t)$ defined for $t \ge 0$ is:
> $$F(s) = \mathcal{L}\{f(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} f(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt$$
> provided the integral converges.

> **Derivation of Unit Step (2.5 marks):** With $f(t) = 1$ for $t \ge 0$:
> $$F(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-st}\, dt = \left[ -\frac{e^{-st}}{s} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left( -\frac{1}{s} \right) = \frac{1}{s}$$

> **Derivation of Unit Ramp (2.5 marks):** With $f(t) = t$:
> $$F(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} t\, e^{-st}\, dt$$
> Integration by parts: $u = t$, $dv = e^{-st} dt \Rightarrow du = dt$, $v = -e^{-st}/s$
> $$F(s) = \left[ -\frac{t e^{-st}}{s} \right]_{0}^{\infty} + \frac{1}{s}\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-st}\, dt = 0 + \frac{1}{s} \cdot \frac{1}{s} = \frac{1}{s^2}$$

#### **Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]**

> **Exponential $e^{-at}$ (3 marks):**
> $$\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at}\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-at} e^{-st}\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(s+a)t}\, dt = \left[ \frac{e^{-(s+a)t}}{-(s+a)} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = \frac{1}{s+a}, \quad s > -a$$

> **Sine $\sin(\omega t)$ (4 marks):** Using Euler's identity:
> $$\sin(\omega t) = \frac{e^{j\omega t} - e^{-j\omega t}}{2j}$$
> $$\mathcal{L}\{\sin(\omega t)\} = \frac{1}{2j} \int_{0}^{\infty} \left[ e^{-(s-j\omega)t} - e^{-(s+j\omega)t} \right] dt$$
> $$= \frac{1}{2j} \left[ \frac{1}{s - j\omega} - \frac{1}{s + j\omega} \right] = \frac{1}{2j} \cdot \frac{2j\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2} = \frac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** What is a unit impulse function? Derive its Laplace transform. Define a translated function and derive the second shifting theorem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Laplace transform of a rectangular pulse of width $h$ and height $1/h$. Hence show that as $h \to 0$, the pulse reduces to a unit impulse. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]**

> **Unit Impulse Definition (2 marks):** The Dirac delta function $\delta(t)$ is a generalized function such that:
> - $\delta(t) = 0$ for $t \ne 0$
> - $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t)\, dt = 1$
> - **Sifting property:** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t) \delta(t - a)\, dt = f(a)$

> **Laplace Transform of Impulse (2 marks):**
> $$\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \delta(t) e^{-st}\, dt = e^{-s(0)} = 1$$

> **Translated Function & Second Shifting Theorem (3 marks):**
> A function $f(t)$ translated (delayed) by $a$ units is $g(t) = f(t-a) u(t-a)$, where $u(t-a)$ is the delayed step.
> $$\mathcal{L}\{f(t-a) u(t-a)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} f(t-a) u(t-a) e^{-st}\, dt = \int_{a}^{\infty} f(t-a) e^{-st}\, dt$$
> Substituting $\tau = t - a$:
> $$= \int_{0}^{\infty} f(\tau) e^{-s(\tau + a)}\, d\tau = e^{-as} F(s)$$

#### **Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]**

> **Pulse Definition (2 marks):** A rectangular pulse of width $h$ and height $1/h$:
> $$p_h(t) = \frac{1}{h}[u(t) - u(t-h)]$$

> **Derivation of Transform (3 marks):**
> $$P(s) = \frac{1}{h}\left[\frac{1}{s} - \frac{e^{-hs}}{s}\right] = \frac{1 - e^{-hs}}{hs}$$

> **Limit as $h \to 0$ (2 marks):** Apply L'Hôpital's rule (form $0/0$):
> $$\lim_{h \to 0} \frac{1 - e^{-hs}}{hs} = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{se^{-hs}}{s} = e^{0} = 1 = \mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\}$$
> ✓ A pulse of vanishing width but unit area converges to a unit impulse.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Missing Region of Convergence (ROC):** Forgetting to state the convergence condition (e.g., $s > -a$ for $e^{-at}$) costs **0.5 to 1 mark**. Always mention ROC.
> 2. **Confusing $f(t)$ with $f(t-a)$:** The second shifting theorem requires the *unit step* multiplier $u(t-a)$: $\mathcal{L}\{f(t-a) u(t-a)\} = e^{-as}F(s)$, **not** $\mathcal{L}\{f(t-a)\} = e^{-as}F(s)$ (the latter is invalid since $f$ may be non-zero for $t < 0$).
> 3. **Wrong sign in s-shift:** $\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at} f(t)\} = F(s+a)$, **not** $F(s-a)$. This sign error is the #1 mistake.
> 4. **Final Value Theorem misuse:** Applying it to systems with poles on the imaginary axis or right-half plane is invalid and results in a **0** for that sub-part.
> 5. **Forgetting Euler's identity:** For sine/cosine derivations, the Euler expansion $e^{j\theta} = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta$ must be used; using the integration-by-parts method twice is acceptable but more time-consuming.
> 6. **Units of $s$:** In process control, $s$ has units of **1/time** (rad/s or s$^{-1}$). Mentioning units earns a bonus mark in KTU viva.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Laplace Transform Basics (Module 1)**

- **Definition:** $F(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} f(t) e^{-st}\, dt$ — a **causal, one-sided** integral transform.
- **Convergence:** Required only if $f(t)$ is piecewise continuous and of exponential order.
- **Linearity:** $\mathcal{L}\{a f + b g\} = aF(s) + bG(s)$ — your **most-used** property.
- **s-shift:** $\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at} f(t)\} = F(s+a)$ — memorize the **positive** sign in $s+a$.
- **Time-shift:** $\mathcal{L}\{f(t-a)u(t-a)\} = e^{-as}F(s)$ — the unit-step multiplier is **non-negotiable**.
- **Differentiation:** $\mathcal{L}\{\dot f(t)\} = sF(s) - f(0)$ — captures **initial conditions**.
- **Integration:** $\mathcal{L}\left\{\int_0^t f(\tau) d\tau\right\} = \dfrac{F(s)}{s}$.
- **Final Value Theorem:** $\lim_{t \to \infty} f(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} sF(s)$ — **only for stable systems**.
- **Initial Value Theorem:** $\lim_{t \to 0^+} f(t) = \lim_{s \to \infty} sF(s)$.
- **Step:** $\mathcal{L}\{1\} = 1/s$ — for sudden setpoint changes.
- **Ramp:** $\mathcal{L}\{t\} = 1/s^2$ — for slow drifts.
- **Exponential:** $\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at}\} = 1/(s+a)$ — for first-order responses.
- **Sine:** $\mathcal{L}\{\sin\omega t\} = \omega/(s^2+\omega^2)$ — for oscillating inputs.
- **Cosine:** $\mathcal{L}\{\cos\omega t\} = s/(s^2+\omega^2)$ — for oscillating inputs.
- **Impulse:** $\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\} = 1$ — for instantaneous shocks.
- **Pulse:** $\mathcal{L}\{p_h(t)\} = (1-e^{-hs})/hs$ — reduces to impulse as $h \to 0$.
- **Translated:** $\mathcal{L}\{f(t-a)u(t-a)\} = e^{-as}F(s)$ — for dead-time processes.
- **Engineering Use:** These transforms are the **building blocks** of every transfer function in classical control.

> **🎯 Pro Tip for KTU Board Exam:** When asked to *find* the Laplace transform, ALWAYS state the function name, write the defining integral, and present the final boxed answer with its region of convergence. This 3-step presentation is what examiners expect for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laplace transform of derivatives and integrals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT601 - PROCESS DYNAMICS CONTROL & INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Laplace transform of derivatives and integrals # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Process Dynamics and Control**, the **Laplace Transform** is an integral transform that converts a time-domain function $f(t)$ (defined for $t \geq 0$) into a complex frequency-domain (s-domain) function $F(s)$. This transformation is the mathematical backbone of linear control system analysis because it converts complex differential equations (which describe dynamic processes) into algebraic equations (which are trivially solvable). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 1):** > The Laplace Transform of a function $f(t)$, defined for $t \geq 0$, is given by: > $$\mathcal{L}\{f(t)\} = F(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} f(t) e^{-st} dt$$ > where $s = \sigma + j\omega$ is a complex variable, provided the integral converges. While the basic transform itself is foundational, its true power in **Process Control** is unlocked by two special properties — the **Laplace Transform of Derivatives** and the **Laplace Transform of Integrals**. These properties allow engineers to model how physical systems (tanks, reactors, heat exchangers) respond to changing inputs. --- ## 1.2 The Two Key Properties Defined ### A. Laplace Transform of the First Derivative If $F(s) = \mathcal{L}\{f(t)\}$, and $f(t)$ is differentiable on $[0, \infty)$, then: $$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{df(t)}{dt}\right\} = sF(s) - f(0)$$ where $f(0)$ is the value of the function at $t = 0$ (the **initial condition**). ### B. Laplace Transform of Higher-Order Derivatives For the $n^{th}$ derivative: $$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{d^n f(t)}{dt^n}\right\} = s^n F(s) - s^{n-1} f(0) - s^{n-2} f'(0) - \cdots - s f^{(n-2)}(0) - f^{(n-1)}(0)$$ ### C. Laplace Transform of an Integral For an integral function $g(t) = \int_{0}^{t} f(\tau) d\tau$: $$\mathcal{L}\left\{\int_{0}^{t} f(\tau) d\tau\right\} = \frac{F(s)}{s}$$ provided the limit $\lim_{t \to \infty} \int_0^t f(\tau) d\tau$ exists. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation (KTU High-Yield Concept):** > - The derivative transform $sF(s)$ represents **how fast a process variable is changing** (e.g., rate of temperature rise in a reactor). > - The integral transform $\frac{F(s)}{s}$ represents **accumulation** (e.g., total mass accumulated in a tank over time). > - The operator $s$ in the s-domain is analogous to the derivative operator $\frac{d}{dt}$ in the time domain. Similarly, $\frac{1}{s}$ is analogous to integration. --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Speedometer and the Odometer" Imagine you are driving a car on a highway. - The **speed of the car** at any moment tells you the **rate of change of distance** with respect to time. This is the **derivative** of position. The Laplace Transform of this derivative gives you a relationship that inherently depends on your **starting position** (your initial condition, $f(0)$). - The **odometer** of the car tells you the **total distance traveled** from the start of the trip. This is the **integral** of speed over time. The Laplace Transform of this integral essentially "divides by $s$" because integration is the inverse operation of differentiation. > [!TIP] > **For KTU students:** Think of $s$ as a "**Differentiator**" and $\frac{1}{s}$ as an "**Integrator**" in the s-domain. Every time you see an $s$ in the numerator, a derivative was involved. Every time you see an $s$ in the denominator, an integration was involved. --- ## 1.4 Boundary Conditions & Convergence For the Laplace Transform to exist, $f(t)$ must be: 1. **Piecewise continuous** on every finite interval in $[0, \infty)$. 2. Be of **exponential order**, meaning there exist real constants $M > 0$, $\alpha > 0$, and $T > 0$ such that $\vert f(t) \vert \leq M e^{\alpha t}$ for all $t \geq T$. The transform integral converges for $\text{Re}(s) > \alpha$, which defines the **Region of Convergence (ROC)**. --- ## 1.5 Initial Condition Classification (Crucial for Control) In **Process Control**, initial conditions are classified as: | Condition Type | Mathematical Form | Physical Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Zero Initial Condition** | $f(0) = 0$ | Tank starts empty; reactor at ambient temperature | | **Non-Zero Initial Condition** | $f(0) \neq 0$ | Tank contains 50 L initially; reactor preheated to $100^\circ C$ | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often drop the $f(0)$ term when taking the derivative's Laplace transform. This is **only valid when initial conditions are zero**. Always state the assumption clearly in your exam answer. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of the Derivative Transform The Laplace Transform of a derivative is derived by applying **integration by parts** to the defining integral. This is the mathematical heart of the topic and must be understood, not memorized. ### Step-by-Step Logic (Why it works): 1. **Start with the definition:** $$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{df}{dt}\right\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{df(t)}{dt} e^{-st} dt$$ 2. **Apply Integration by Parts** with $u = e^{-st}$ and $dv = \frac{df}{dt} dt$: - $du = -s e^{-st} dt$ - $v = f(t)$ Using $\int u \, dv = uv - \int v \, du$: $$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{df}{dt}\right\} = \left[f(t) e^{-st}\right]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} f(t) (-s e^{-st}) dt$$ 3. **Evaluate the boundary term** as $t \to \infty$: assuming $f(t)$ grows slower than $e^{st}$, the term vanishes. At $t = 0$, the term is $-f(0)$. 4. **Simplify the remaining integral:** $$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{df}{dt}\right\} = -f(0) + s \int_{0}^{\infty} f(t) e^{-st} dt = sF(s) - f(0)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for control:** Because the derivative transform *embeds the initial condition* directly into the algebraic equation, you never have to solve a differential equation from scratch with constants of integration. You simply substitute $f(0)$, $f'(0)$, etc., and solve the resulting algebra. --- ## 2.2 Theoretical Breakdown of the Integral Transform The Laplace Transform of an integral is derived using the **convolution property** or, more directly, by recognizing that integration is the **inverse operation** of differentiation. ### The Logic: Let $g(t) = \int_0^t f(\tau) d\tau$. Then by the **Fundamental Theorem of Calculus**: $$\frac{dg(t)}{dt} = f(t) \quad \text{with} \quad g(0) = 0$$ Now apply the derivative transform property to $g(t)$: $$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{dg(t)}{dt}\right\} = sG(s) - g(0)$$ Since $g(0) = 0$ (the integral from 0 to 0 is zero): $$\mathcal{L}\{f(t)\} = sG(s)$$ Solving for $G(s)$: $$G(s) = \frac{F(s)}{s} \implies \mathcal{L}\left\{\int_0^t f(\tau) d\tau\right\} = \frac{F(s)}{s}$$ --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Master Table — Memorize These** | # | Property | Time-Domain Function $f(t)$ | Laplace Transform $F(s)$ | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **First Derivative** | $\dfrac{df(t)}{dt}$ | $sF(s) - f(0)$ | | 2 | **Second Derivative** | $\dfrac{d^2 f(t)}{dt^2}$ | $s^2 F(s) - s f(0) - f'(0)$ | | 3 | **$n^{th}$ Derivative** | $\dfrac{d^n f(t)}{dt^n}$ | $s^n F(s) - \sum_{k=1}^{n} s^{n-k} f^{(k-1)}(0)$ | | 4 | **Definite Integral** | $\displaystyle\int_{0}^{t} f(\tau) d\tau$ | $\dfrac{F(s)}{s}$ | | 5 | **Indefinite Integral** | $\displaystyle\int f(t) dt$ | $\dfrac{F(s)}{s} + \dfrac{1}{s} \int f(0) dt$ | | 6 | **Unit Step** | $u(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{s}$ | | 7 | **Ramp** | $t$ | $\dfrac{1}{s^2}$ | | 8 | **Exponential** | $e^{-at}$ | $\dfrac{1}{s+a}$ | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL Notation Alert:** In property 3, the term $f^{(k-1)}(0)$ denotes the $(k-1)^{th}$ derivative of $f$ evaluated at $t=0$. This is the standard KTU textbook notation. Always write out the full expansion for clarity during exams. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Area | Use of Derivative Transform | Use of Integral Transform | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Chemical Reactor Control** | Determines rate of concentration change | Calculates total reactant consumed | | **Liquid Level Systems** | Models rate of level change in tanks | Tracks cumulative volume in/out | | **Thermal Systems** | Predicts heating/cooling rates | Computes total heat transferred | | **PID Controller Design** | Transfer functions use $s$ as derivative operator | $\frac{1}{s}$ represents integral action | > [!TIP] > **For KTU Process Control:** In deriving the transfer function of a stirred-tank heater, you will encounter terms like $\frac{dT}{dt}$ (rate of temperature change) and $\int (T_{in} - T) dt$ (accumulated temperature difference). These transform to $sT(s) - T(0)$ and $\frac{T_{in}(s) - T(s)}{s}$ respectively — directly enabling algebraic manipulation. --- ## 2.5 Initial Value & Final Value Theorems (Companion Concepts) > [!NOTE] > These theorems are direct consequences of the derivative/integral transform properties and are frequently asked in KTU exams. ### A. Initial Value Theorem (IVT): $$\lim_{t \to 0^+} f(t) = \lim_{s \to \infty} sF(s)$$ *Valid only if the limit exists.* ### B. Final Value Theorem (FVT): $$\lim_{t \to \infty} f(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} sF(s)$$ *Valid only if all poles of $sF(s)$ have negative real parts (system is stable).* > [!WARNING] > The FVT fails if $sF(s)$ has poles on the imaginary axis (e.g., $s=0$). KTU students often lose marks by applying FVT to marginally stable systems. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Laplace Transform of the First Derivative **Objective:** Prove that $\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{df(t)}{dt}\right\} = sF(s) - f(0)$. --- **Starting Point (Definition):** $$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{df(t)}{dt}\right\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{df(t)}{dt} \, e^{-st} dt$$ --- **Step 1 — Identify the parts for Integration by Parts:** Recall the formula: $\int u \, dv = uv - \int v \, du$ Let us choose: - $u = e^{-st}$ → so $du = -s e^{-st} \, dt$ - $dv = \dfrac{df(t)}{dt} \, dt = df(t)$ → so $v = f(t)$ --- **Step 2 — Substitute into Integration by Parts:** $$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{df(t)}{dt} e^{-st} dt = \left[e^{-st} f(t)\right]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} f(t) \left(-s e^{-st}\right) dt$$ --- **Step 3 — Evaluate the boundary term:** $$\left[e^{-st} f(t)\right]_{0}^{\infty} = \lim_{t \to \infty} e^{-st} f(t) - e^{0} f(0)$$ For convergence of the Laplace transform, we require $\lim_{t \to \infty} e^{-st} f(t) = 0$ (for $\text{Re}(s) > \alpha$). So the boundary term simplifies to: $$0 - f(0) = -f(0)$$ --- **Step 4 — Simplify the integral term:** $$- \int_{0}^{\infty} f(t) \left(-s e^{-st}\right) dt = +s \int_{0}^{\infty} f(t) e^{-st} dt = s F(s)$$ --- **Step 5 — Combine results:** $$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{df(t)}{dt}\right\} = -f(0) + sF(s) = sF(s) - f(0)$$ $$\boxed{\therefore \mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{df(t)}{dt}\right\} = sF(s) - f(0)}$$ --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Laplace Transform of the Second Derivative **Objective:** Prove that $\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{d^2 f(t)}{dt^2}\right\} = s^2 F(s) - s f(0) - f'(0)$. --- **Step 1 — Apply the first derivative result:** Let $g(t) = \dfrac{df(t)}{dt}$. Then $\dfrac{dg(t)}{dt} = \dfrac{d^2 f(t)}{dt^2}$. By the first derivative formula: $$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{dg(t)}{dt}\right\} = sG(s) - g(0)$$ Substituting back: $$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{d^2 f(t)}{dt^2}\right\} = s \, \mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{df(t)}{dt}\right\} - f'(0)$$ --- **Step 2 — Substitute the first derivative transform:** We know $\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{df(t)}{dt}\right\} = sF(s) - f(0)$. Therefore: $$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{d^2 f(t)}{dt^2}\right\} = s [sF(s) - f(0)] - f'(0)$$ --- **Step 3 — Expand and simplify:** $$= s^2 F(s) - s f(0) - f'(0)$$ $$\boxed{\therefore \mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{d^2 f(t)}{dt^2}\right\} = s^2 F(s) - s f(0) - f'(0)}$$ --- ## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: Laplace Transform of an Integral **Objective:** Prove that $\mathcal{L}\left\{\int_0^t f(\tau) d\tau\right\} = \dfrac{F(s)}{s}$. --- **Step 1 — Define the integral function:** Let $g(t) = \displaystyle\int_0^t f(\tau) d\tau$. By the **Fundamental Theorem of Calculus**: $$\frac{dg(t)}{dt} = f(t) \quad \text{and} \quad g(0) = \int_0^0 f(\tau) d\tau = 0$$ --- **Step 2 — Apply the derivative transform to $g(t)$:** $$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{dg(t)}{dt}\right\} = s G(s) - g(0)$$ Substituting: $$\mathcal{L}\{f(t)\} = s G(s) - 0 = s G(s)$$ --- **Step 3 — Solve for $G(s)$:** $$F(s) = s G(s) \implies G(s) = \frac{F(s)}{s}$$ --- **Step 4 — Final result:** $$\boxed{\therefore \mathcal{L}\left\{\int_0^t f(\tau) d\tau\right\} = \frac{F(s)}{s}}$$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example — Derivative Transform > [!NOTE] > **KTU Typical Exam Problem:** Find the Laplace Transform of $f(t) = \sin(at)$ using the derivative transform property. --- **Given:** $f(t) = \sin(at)$, so $f(0) = \sin(0) = 0$, and $f'(0) = a\cos(0) = a$. **Compute the second derivative:** $$f'(t) = a\cos(at)$$ $$f''(t) = -a^2 \sin(at) = -a^2 f(t)$$ --- **Step 1 — Take the Laplace transform of both sides:** $$\mathcal{L}\{f''(t)\} = -a^2 \mathcal{L}\{f(t)\}$$ --- **Step 2 — Apply the second derivative formula:** $$s^2 F(s) - s f(0) - f'(0) = -a^2 F(s)$$ Substitute the initial conditions: $$s^2 F(s) - s(0) - a = -a^2 F(s)$$ $$s^2 F(s) - a = -a^2 F(s)$$ --- **Step 3 — Collect terms with $F(s)$:** $$s^2 F(s) + a^2 F(s) = a$$ $$F(s) (s^2 + a^2) = a$$ --- **Step 4 — Solve for $F(s)$:** $$F(s) = \frac{a}{s^2 + a^2}$$ This matches the standard KTU table result: $\mathcal{L}\{\sin(at)\} = \dfrac{a}{s^2+a^2}$. ✓ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Worked Example — Integral Transform > [!NOTE] > **KTU Typical Exam Problem:** Find $\mathcal{L}\{t^2\}$ using the integral transform property. --- **Step 1 — Express $t^2$ as an integral:** Recall that $\dfrac{d}{dt}\left(\dfrac{t^3}{3}\right) = t^2$. So: $$t^2 = \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{t^3}{3}\right) = \int_0^t 2\tau \, d\tau$$ Using the integral property: $$\mathcal{L}\left\{\int_0^t f(\tau) d\tau\right\} = \frac{F(s)}{s}$$ Here, $f(\tau) = 2\tau$, so $F(s) = \mathcal{L}\{2\tau\} = \dfrac{2}{s^2}$. --- **Step 2 — Apply the property:** $$\mathcal{L}\{t^2\} = \frac{F(s)}{s} = \frac{2/s^2}{s} = \frac{2}{s^3}$$ $$\boxed{\therefore \mathcal{L}\{t^2\} = \frac{2}{s^3}}$$ This matches the standard result: $\mathcal{L}\{t^n\} = \dfrac{n!}{s^{n+1}}$. ✓ --- ## 3.6 Worked Example — Solving an ODE (The Real Power) > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the primary application tested in KTU Process Control exams.** **Problem:** Solve $\dfrac{d^2 y}{dt^2} + 5\dfrac{dy}{dt} + 6y = 0$, with $y(0) = 2$, $y'(0) = 3$. --- **Step 1 — Take the Laplace transform of both sides:** Using $\mathcal{L}\{y''\} = s^2 Y(s) - s y(0) - y'(0)$ and $\mathcal{L}\{y'\} = sY(s) - y(0)$: $$[s^2 Y(s) - 2s - 3] + 5[sY(s) - 2] + 6Y(s) = 0$$ --- **Step 2 — Expand and collect $Y(s)$ terms:** $$s^2 Y(s) - 2s - 3 + 5sY(s) - 10 + 6Y(s) = 0$$ $$Y(s)(s^2 + 5s + 6) = 2s + 13$$ --- **Step 3 — Factor and solve for $Y(s)$:** $$Y(s) = \frac{2s + 13}{(s+2)(s+3)}$$ --- **Step 4 — Partial Fraction Decomposition:** $$Y(s) = \frac{A}{s+2} + \frac{B}{s+3}$$ Solving: $A = 9$, $B = -7$. $$Y(s) = \frac{9}{s+2} - \frac{7}{s+3}$$ --- **Step 5 — Take the Inverse Laplace Transform:** $$\boxed{y(t) = 9 e^{-2t} - 7 e^{-3t}}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** Notice how initial conditions $y(0)=2$ and $y'(0)=3$ entered the equation algebraically without any integration constants. This is the core reason Laplace transforms are preferred in control engineering. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Flow: From Time Domain to s-Domain This Mermaid diagram illustrates how the derivative and integral transforms act as **bridges** between the time and frequency domains. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["TIME DOMAIN
f of t"] nodeB["DIFFERENTIATION
d by dt"] nodeC["INTEGRATION
integral from 0 to t"] nodeD["S DOMAIN
F of s"] nodeA -->|"Apply L"| nodeD nodeD -->|"Multiply by s"| nodeE["s F of s minus f 0"] nodeD -->|"Divide by s"| nodeF["F of s divided by s"] nodeB -->|"L gives"| nodeE nodeC -->|"L gives"| nodeF nodeE -.->|"Inverse L"| nodeB nodeF -.->|"Inverse L"| nodeC style nodeA fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc style nodeD fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600 style nodeE fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000 style nodeF fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600 ``` --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Solving ODEs via Laplace This Mermaid diagram depicts the **engineering workflow** when using derivative/integral transforms to solve a process control ODE. ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA["STEP 1: Write ODE
in Time Domain"] stepB["STEP 2: Apply L to
each derivative term"] stepC["STEP 3: Substitute
Initial Conditions"] stepD["STEP 4: Rearrange into
Algebraic Equation"] stepE["STEP 5: Solve Algebraically
for Y of s"] stepF["STEP 6: Partial Fraction
Decomposition"] stepG["STEP 7: Apply Inverse L
to get y of t"] stepA -->|"L of y double prime
L of y prime
L of y"| stepB stepB -->|"s squared Y minus s y0 minus y prime 0"| stepC stepC -->|"Insert numerical IC values"| stepD stepD -->|"Collect Y of s terms"| stepE stepE -->|"Express as rational function"| stepF stepF -->|"Match with standard transform pairs"| stepG style stepA fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc style stepC fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000 style stepG fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600 ``` --- ## 4.3 Property Mapping Matrix (Mermaid Block Diagram) This diagram maps each property to its application domain in chemical process control. ```mermaid flowchart TB propA["Property 1: L of df by dt
equals s F of s minus f of 0"] propB["Property 2: L of d2f by dt2
equals s squared F of s minus s f of 0 minus f prime of 0"] propC["Property 3: L of integral from 0 to t f tau d tau
equals F of s divided by s"] appA["App 1: Level Tank Dynamics
dh by dt equals Fin minus Fout by A"] appB["App 2: CSTR Temperature
dT by dt involves multiple rates"] appC["App 3: Accumulator Volume
V equals integral of flow dt"] propA -->|"Enables modeling of"| appA propB -->|"Enables modeling of"| appB propC -->|"Enables modeling of"| appC style propA fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000 style propB fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600 style propC fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600 ``` --- ## 4.4 S-Domain Operator Analogy Diagram This diagram reinforces the **conceptual analogy** between time-domain operators and s-domain operators. ```mermaid flowchart LR timeOp["TIME DOMAIN OPERATORS"] sOp["S DOMAIN OPERATORS"] timeOp -->|"d by dt"| dOp["Differentiation"] timeOp -->|"Integral from 0 to t"| iOp["Integration"] sOp -->|"Multiply by s"| sOpMult["Multiplication by s"] sOp -->|"Divide by s"| sOpDiv["Division by s"] dOp -.->|"Maps to"| sOpMult iOp -.->|"Maps to"| sOpDiv style timeOp fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc style sOp fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600 ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Memory Aid:** "**D**ifferentiation in time $\to$ **M**ultiplication by $s$ in s-domain" (DM rule). And "**I**ntegration in time $\to$ **D**ivision by $s$ in s-domain" (ID rule). # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > These questions test **CO1 (Apply knowledge of mathematics, science, and engineering fundamentals)** at Bloom's Level 2 (Understand). --- ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State and prove the Laplace transform of the first derivative of a function $f(t)$.** **Model Answer:** > **Statement:** If $F(s) = \mathcal{L}\{f(t)\}$, then $\mathcal{L}\left\{\dfrac{df(t)}{dt}\right\} = sF(s) - f(0)$, where $f(0)$ is the initial value of $f(t)$ at $t = 0$. > > **Proof:** By definition, > $$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{df}{dt}\right\} = \int_0^\infty \frac{df}{dt} e^{-st} dt$$ > > Using integration by parts with $u = e^{-st}$, $dv = df$: > $$= \left[f(t)e^{-st}\right]_0^\infty + s\int_0^\infty f(t) e^{-st} dt$$ > $$= -f(0) + sF(s) = sF(s) - f(0)$$ > > Hence proved. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **State the Laplace transform of an integral. Why is it useful in process control?** **Model Answer:** > **Statement:** $\mathcal{L}\left\{\displaystyle\int_0^t f(\tau) d\tau\right\} = \dfrac{F(s)}{s}$ > > **Use in Process Control:** > 1. It converts cumulative/accumulation processes (like total volume flow or total heat transfer) into simple algebraic expressions. > 2. It enables the derivation of **transfer functions** for integral-type controllers (e.g., the I-action in a PID controller is represented by $\frac{1}{s}$ in the s-domain). > 3. It allows modeling of **level systems** where the output is the integral of net inflow rate. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do NOT** drop the $f(0)$ term in the derivative transform unless explicitly told "zero initial conditions." > - **Always** show integration by parts steps when asked to "derive" — partial marks are awarded for each correct algebraic transition. > - For integral transforms, **always state** that $g(0) = 0$ before applying the derivative property. > - In partial fraction decomposition, **double-check** your $A$ and $B$ coefficients by substitution. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **(a)** Derive the Laplace transform of the second derivative of $f(t)$, i.e., $\mathcal{L}\left\{\dfrac{d^2 f(t)}{dt^2}\right\}$. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using the derivative transform properties, derive the Laplace transform of $\cos(at)$ given that $\mathcal{L}\{\sin(at)\} = \dfrac{a}{s^2 + a^2}$. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question A:** **(a) Derivation:** > **Assumption:** $f(t)$ is piecewise continuous on $[0, \infty)$ and of exponential order. Let $g(t) = \dfrac{df(t)}{dt}$. Then $\dfrac{dg}{dt} = \dfrac{d^2 f}{dt^2}$. By the first derivative formula: $$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{dg}{dt}\right\} = sG(s) - g(0)$$ But $G(s) = \mathcal{L}\{f'(t)\} = sF(s) - f(0)$, and $g(0) = f'(0)$. Substituting: $$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{d^2 f}{dt^2}\right\} = s[sF(s) - f(0)] - f'(0)$$ $$\boxed{\therefore \mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{d^2 f}{dt^2}\right\} = s^2 F(s) - s f(0) - f'(0)}$$ **(b) Derivation of $\mathcal{L}\{\cos(at)\}$:** Differentiate $\sin(at)$ twice: $$\frac{d}{dt}\sin(at) = a\cos(at)$$ $$\frac{d^2}{dt^2}\sin(at) = -a^2 \sin(at)$$ Apply Laplace transform to both sides: $$\mathcal{L}\{f''(t)\} = -a^2 \mathcal{L}\{\sin(at)\} = -a^2 \cdot \frac{a}{s^2 + a^2}$$ Using the second derivative formula with $f(t) = \sin(at)$, $f(0) = 0$, $f'(0) = a$: $$s^2 F(s) - s(0) - a = -\frac{a^3}{s^2 + a^2}$$ $$s^2 F(s) = a - \frac{a^3}{s^2 + a^2} = \frac{a(s^2 + a^2) - a^3}{s^2 + a^2} = \frac{a s^2}{s^2 + a^2}$$ $$F(s) = \frac{a s^2}{s^2(s^2 + a^2)} = \frac{s}{s^2 + a^2}$$ $$\boxed{\therefore \mathcal{L}\{\cos(at)\} = \frac{s}{s^2 + a^2}}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** State and prove the Laplace transform of the integral $\displaystyle\int_0^t f(\tau) d\tau$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Solve the differential equation $\dfrac{d^2 y}{dt^2} + 4\dfrac{dy}{dt} + 3y = e^{-t}$, given $y(0) = 1$, $y'(0) = 0$, using Laplace transforms. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question B:** **(a) Proof of Integral Transform:** Let $g(t) = \displaystyle\int_0^t f(\tau) d\tau$. By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus: $$\frac{dg(t)}{dt} = f(t), \quad g(0) = 0$$ Applying the derivative transform: $$\mathcal{L}\{f(t)\} = sG(s) - g(0) = sG(s)$$ $$\implies G(s) = \frac{F(s)}{s}$$ $$\boxed{\therefore \mathcal{L}\left\{\int_0^t f(\tau) d\tau\right\} = \frac{F(s)}{s}}$$ **(b) Solving the ODE:** Take Laplace transform of both sides. Using $y(0) = 1$, $y'(0) = 0$: $$[s^2 Y(s) - s(1) - 0] + 4[sY(s) - 1] + 3Y(s) = \frac{1}{s+1}$$ $$Y(s)[s^2 + 4s + 3] - s - 4 = \frac{1}{s+1}$$ $$Y(s)(s+1)(s+3) = s + 4 + \frac{1}{s+1} = \frac{(s+4)(s+1) + 1}{s+1} = \frac{s^2 + 5s + 5}{s+1}$$ $$Y(s) = \frac{s^2 + 5s + 5}{(s+1)^2(s+3)}$$ **Partial Fraction Decomposition:** $$Y(s) = \frac{A}{s+1} + \frac{B}{(s+1)^2} + \frac{C}{s+3}$$ Multiplying through: $$s^2 + 5s + 5 = A(s+1)(s+3) + B(s+3) + C(s+1)^2$$ Set $s = -1$: $1 - 5 + 5 = B(2) \implies B = \frac{1}{2}$ Set $s = -3$: $9 - 15 + 5 = C(4) \implies C = -\frac{1}{4}$ Compare $s^2$ coefficients: $1 = A + C \implies A = 1 + \frac{1}{4} = \frac{5}{4}$ So: $$Y(s) = \frac{5/4}{s+1} + \frac{1/2}{(s+1)^2} - \frac{1/4}{s+3}$$ **Inverse Laplace Transform:** Using $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{s+a}\right\} = e^{-at}$ and $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{(s+a)^2}\right\} = t e^{-at}$: $$\boxed{y(t) = \frac{5}{4} e^{-t} + \frac{1}{2} t e^{-t} - \frac{1}{4} e^{-3t}}$$ --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam** - [x] **First Derivative:** $\mathcal{L}\{f'(t)\} = sF(s) - f(0)$ — always include the initial condition. - [x] **Second Derivative:** $\mathcal{L}\{f''(t)\} = s^2 F(s) - s f(0) - f'(0)$ — has TWO initial conditions. - [x] **$n^{th}$ Derivative:** General formula includes $n$ initial condition terms. - [x] **Integral:** $\mathcal{L}\left\{\int_0^t f(\tau) d\tau\right\} = \dfrac{F(s)}{s}$ — derived from the inverse relationship with differentiation. - [x] **Zero IC shortcut:** When $f(0) = f'(0) = \cdots = 0$, the derivative transform reduces to $s^n F(s)$. - [x] **DM-ID Rule:** Differentiation $\leftrightarrow$ Multiplication by $s$; Integration $\leftrightarrow$ Division by $s$. - [x] **Proof Method:** Always use **Integration by Parts** for derivative transform; use **Fundamental Theorem of Calculus** for integral transform. - [x] **Convergence:** Requires $f(t)$ to be of exponential order; ROC is $\text{Re}(s) > \alpha$. - [x] **Applications in KTU:** Transfer function derivation, solving ODEs with initial conditions, modeling of tank/reactor dynamics, PID controller representation. > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Tip:** When a question says "using Laplace transform, solve the ODE," you must: (1) Transform each term, (2) Substitute initial conditions as numerical values, (3) Rearrange algebraically, (4) Partial fraction decompose, and (5) Apply inverse transform table. Skipping any step costs 2–3 marks.

topics.title: Initial value theorem and final value theorem; Numerical problems and theoretical model development of a tank heater system - input-output model and its solution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT601 - PROCESS DYNAMICS CONTROL & INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Initial value theorem and final value theorem; Numerical problems and theoretical model development of a tank heater system - input-output model and its solution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Initial Value Theorem (IVT)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
The **Initial Value Theorem** states that if $F(s) = \mathcal{L}\{f(t)\}$ and if the limit exists, then the initial value of a time-domain function $f(t)$ (as $t \to 0^+$) can be directly computed from its Laplace transform $F(s)$ by evaluating:

$$f(0^+) = \lim_{t \to 0^+} f(t) = \lim_{s \to \infty} s F(s)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $0^+$ and not $0$?**
> In process control, we deal with signals that may have discontinuities at $t=0$ (e.g., step inputs). The "+" superscript denotes the value *immediately after* the input is applied, which is the physically meaningful quantity for causal systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Examiner's Pet Topic:**
> IVT is the fastest way to check whether a transfer function model has an **instantaneous response** (non-zero $f(0^+)$) or a **delayed response** (zero $f(0^+)$). This is crucial for classifying systems as *Type-0, Type-1, or higher*.

---

## 1.2 Final Value Theorem (FVT)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
The **Final Value Theorem** states that if all poles of $sF(s)$ lie strictly in the **left-half of the s-plane** (i.e., $\text{Re}(p_i) < 0$ for all poles $p_i$ of $sF(s)$), then:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} f(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s F(s)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Critical Restriction:**
> The FVT **CANNOT be applied** if:
> 1. $F(s)$ has poles on the imaginary axis (e.g., pure integrators, oscillators).
> 2. $F(s)$ has poles in the right-half plane (unstable systems).
>
> In such cases, the limit $\lim_{t\to\infty} f(t)$ either does not exist or is infinite, and applying FVT will yield a *mathematically incorrect finite value*. KTU examiners frequently test this pitfall.

---

## 1.3 Tank Heater System — Conceptual Overview

A **stirred-tank heater** is a canonical first-order process in chemical engineering. A liquid stream enters the tank, is heated (or cooled) by a utility stream (steam or cooling water), and leaves with a modified temperature.

**Physical Setup:**

- **Inlet stream:** Volumetric flow rate $F$ at temperature $T_i$ (the *process input* that may vary).
- **Tank contents:** Volume $V$, density $\rho$, heat capacity $C_p$, perfectly mixed, temperature $T$ (the *output*).
- **Heating utility:** Heat transfer rate $Q$ supplied by a jacket/coil.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Tea Kettle":**
> Imagine boiling water in a kettle. The temperature of the water ($T$) is your **output**. You can change the *input* in two ways: (1) change the flame (heat input $Q$), or (2) add cold water (change inlet flow $F$ or inlet temperature $T_i$). The kettle has a *thermal inertia* — it takes time to heat up, never reaching the desired temperature instantly. This inertia is the **time constant** $\tau$ of the system. IVT tells you the water is still at room temperature the instant you light the stove (zero initial jump). FVT tells you the eventual steady-state temperature after the water has boiled for a long time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:**
> The tank heater belongs to the family of **lumped parameter systems** — properties (like $T$) are assumed uniform throughout the control volume. This converts a *distributed* physical problem into an *ordinary differential equation (ODE)*, which is then solved using Laplace transforms.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Initial Value Theorem — Deep Dive

**Logical Steps:**

1. Start from the Laplace transform of the time derivative:
$$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{df(t)}{dt}\right\} = sF(s) - f(0^+)$$

2. Take the limit as $s \to \infty$. The derivative term vanishes if $f(t)$ is well-behaved, leaving:
$$\lim_{s \to \infty} \left[ sF(s) - f(0^+) \right] = 0$$

3. Rearrange to isolate the initial value:
$$f(0^+) = \lim_{s \to \infty} sF(s)$$

**Physical Interpretation:**

- A non-zero initial value indicates the system exhibits an **instantaneous (proper) response** — energy/mass transport that does not require accumulation.
- A zero initial value indicates **strictly proper behavior** — pure first-order or higher dynamics with no direct feed-through.

> [!NOTE]
> **Connection to System Classification:**
> If $G(s) = \frac{b_m s^m + \cdots + b_0}{a_n s^n + \cdots + a_0}$ and $m = n$ (equal order of numerator and denominator), then $g(0^+) = b_m/a_n$ (a non-zero direct transmission). If $m < n$, the system is *strictly proper* and $g(0^+) = 0$. KTU problems often ask you to deduce this without doing the inverse Laplace transform.

---

## 2.2 The Final Value Theorem — Deep Dive

**Logical Steps:**

1. Again, start from the derivative formula:
$$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{df(t)}{dt}\right\} = sF(s) - f(0^+)$$

2. Take the limit as $s \to 0$. The Laplace operator $s$ acts as a "low-pass filter," selecting only the long-term DC behavior:
$$\lim_{s \to 0} \left[ sF(s) - f(0^+) \right] = \lim_{t \to \infty} \left[ f(t) - f(0^+) \right]$$

3. Under the stability condition (all poles of $sF(s)$ in LHP), the time-domain limit exists and simplifies to:
$$\lim_{t \to \infty} f(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} sF(s)$$

**Failure Modes — Where FVT Breaks Down:**

| Scenario | Example $F(s)$ | Why FVT Fails | True $\lim_{t \to \infty} f(t)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pole at $s=0$ | $\dfrac{1}{s(s+1)}$ | $sF(s) = \frac{1}{s+1}$ has pole at origin | $\infty$ (ramp-like growth) |
| Poles on $j\omega$ axis | $\dfrac{1}{s^2 + 1}$ | Undamped oscillation | Does not exist (oscillates) |
| Pole in RHP | $\dfrac{1}{s-1}$ | $sF(s) = \frac{s}{s-1}$ unstable | $\infty$ (exponential blow-up) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> FVT is the **workhorse for computing steady-state errors** in feedback control. By evaluating $\lim_{s \to 0} sE(s)$ where $E(s)$ is the error signal, control engineers can predict offset without solving time-domain ODEs — a critical design tool in the **Frequency Response** module that follows in this course.

---

## 2.3 Tank Heater — System Anatomy

**Degrees of Freedom (Inputs that can be manipulated):**

1. **Inlet flow rate** $F$ — affects residence time.
2. **Inlet temperature** $T_i$ — disturbs the energy balance.
3. **Heat input rate** $Q$ — manipulated by steam valve.

**Output (Controlled Variable):**

- **Tank temperature** $T$ — the variable we want to regulate.

**Assumptions (must be stated in any KTU answer):**

1. Perfect mixing (lumped parameter).
2. Constant density $\rho$ and heat capacity $C_p$ (weakly temperature-dependent liquids).
3. Constant liquid volume $V$ (outlet flow adjusted to match inlet).
4. Heat losses to surroundings are negligible OR lumped into $Q$.
5. Single liquid phase, no phase change.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initial Value Theorem (IVT) | $f(0^+) = \lim_{s \to \infty} sF(s)$ | Valid for any causal $f(t)$ with well-defined limit |
| 2 | Final Value Theorem (FVT) | $\lim_{t \to \infty} f(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} sF(s)$ | **Stability prerequisite:** poles of $sF(s)$ in LHP |
| 3 | First-Order Standard Form | $\tau \dfrac{dy}{dt} + y = K u(t)$ | $y$: output, $u$: input, $\tau$: time constant (s), $K$: gain (K) |
| 4 | First-Order Transfer Function | $G(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$ | Derived from standard form via Laplace |
| 5 | Step Response (First-Order) | $y(t) = K \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)$ | $y(0^+) = 0$ (IVT), $y(\infty) = K$ (FVT) |
| 6 | Tank Heater Time Constant | $\tau = \dfrac{V \rho C_p}{F_s \rho C_p + UA}$ | $V$ in m³, $F$ in m³/s, $UA$ in W/K |
| 7 | Tank Heater Gain w.r.t. $T_i$ | $K_1 = \dfrac{F_s \rho C_p}{F_s \rho C_p + UA}$ | Dimensionless if $T$ in K |
| 8 | Tank Heater Gain w.r.t. $F$ | $K_2 = \dfrac{(T_{i,s} - T_s) \rho C_p}{F_s \rho C_p + UA}$ | Units: K per (m³/s) |
| 9 | Generalized Energy Balance | $\dfrac{dU}{dt} = \dot{m}_{in} h_{in} - \dot{m}_{out} h_{out} + Q - W$ | First Law of Thermodynamics for open system |
| 10 | Step Response Initial Value | $y(0^+) = 0$ (strictly proper first-order) | Confirms IVT for $G(s) = K/(\tau s + 1)$ |
| 11 | Step Response Final Value | $y(\infty) = K$ | Confirms FVT for $G(s) = K/(\tau s + 1)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility Beyond KTU:**
> The tank heater model is the template for **heat exchangers, CSTRs, mixing tanks, and jacketed reactors** in real plant design. Companies like Honeywell, Emerson (DeltaV), and Siemens use this exact first-order-plus-dead-time (FOPDT) structure when auto-tuning industrial PID controllers in DCS systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Initial Value Theorem

**Starting Point:** The Laplace transform of the first derivative of $f(t)$ is:

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{ \frac{df(t)}{dt} \right\} = \int_{0^-}^{\infty} \frac{df(t)}{dt} \, e^{-st} \, dt = sF(s) - f(0^-)$$

Using integration by parts:

$$= \left[ f(t) e^{-st} \right]_{0^-}^{\infty} + s \int_{0^-}^{\infty} f(t) e^{-st} \, dt$$

$$= \lim_{t \to \infty} f(t) e^{-st} - f(0^-) + sF(s)$$

**Step 1:** For $f(t)$ of exponential order (i.e., $|f(t)| \le M e^{at}$ for some finite $M, a$), the term $\lim_{t \to \infty} f(t) e^{-st}$ vanishes as $s \to \infty$, since $e^{-st} \to 0$ dominates any exponential growth of $f(t)$ when $s$ is large enough.

$$0 - f(0^-) + \lim_{s \to \infty} sF(s) = \lim_{s \to \infty} \mathcal{L}\left\{ \frac{df}{dt} \right\}$$

**Step 2:** The Laplace transform of a bounded derivative also vanishes as $s \to \infty$ (a standard result from transform theory). Therefore:

$$- f(0^-) + \lim_{s \to \infty} sF(s) = 0$$

**Step 3:** Rearranging (and using the physical convention $f(0^+)$ for the value *after* any discontinuity at $t=0$):

$$\boxed{f(0^+) = \lim_{s \to \infty} sF(s)}$$

> This completes the derivation of IVT. ∎

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Final Value Theorem

**Starting Point:** The same derivative formula:

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{ \frac{df(t)}{dt} \right\} = sF(s) - f(0^-)$$

**Step 1:** Take the limit as $s \to 0$ on both sides. The Laplace transform of the derivative becomes:

$$\lim_{s \to 0} \int_{0^-}^{\infty} \frac{df}{dt} e^{-st} dt = \int_{0^-}^{\infty} \frac{df}{dt} \cdot 1 \, dt = f(\infty) - f(0^-)$$

**Step 2:** Substituting back into the Laplace equation:

$$f(\infty) - f(0^-) = \lim_{s \to 0} \left[ sF(s) - f(0^-) \right]$$

**Step 3:** The $f(0^-)$ terms cancel on both sides:

$$f(\infty) = \lim_{s \to 0} sF(s)$$

**Step 4 (Stability Caveat):** The interchange of the limit $s \to 0$ with the integration requires that the integrand $\frac{df}{dt} e^{-st}$ be uniformly bounded. This holds **only if** all poles of $sF(s)$ are strictly in the open left-half plane. If a pole sits at $s=0$ or in the RHP, $f(\infty)$ either diverges or does not exist, and the equation above is invalid.

$$\boxed{\lim_{t \to \infty} f(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} sF(s) \quad \text{subject to stability}}$$

> This completes the derivation of FVT. ∎

---

## 3.3 Numerical Problem Set 1 — IVT and FVT Application

### **Problem 1: Compute the initial and final values of a process variable**

Given the Laplace-domain output of a process:
$$Y(s) = \frac{5}{s(s+2)(s+3)}$$

Determine $y(0^+)$ and $y(\infty)$ using IVT and FVT. **Verify stability** first.

**Solution:**

**Step A — Check Stability:** The poles are at $s = 0, -2, -3$. There is a pole at the origin. For FVT, we need all poles of $sY(s)$ in the LHP.

$$sY(s) = \frac{5}{(s+2)(s+3)}$$

The poles of $sY(s)$ are at $s = -2$ and $s = -3$ — both in the LHP. **FVT is applicable.**

**Step B — Initial Value via IVT:**

$$y(0^+) = \lim_{s \to \infty} sY(s) = \lim_{s \to \infty} \frac{5}{(s+2)(s+3)} = 0$$

$$\boxed{y(0^+) = 0}$$

**Step C — Final Value via FVT:**

$$y(\infty) = \lim_{s \to 0} sY(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{5}{(s+2)(s+3)} = \frac{5}{(2)(3)} = \frac{5}{6}$$

$$\boxed{y(\infty) = \frac{5}{6} \approx 0.833}$$

---

### **Problem 2: Identify when FVT must NOT be used**

Given $Y(s) = \dfrac{2}{s^2 + 1}$, determine whether FVT may be applied.

**Solution:**

The poles are at $s = \pm j$, both on the imaginary axis. $sY(s) = \dfrac{2s}{s^2 + 1}$ has poles *still* on the imaginary axis. **FVT is NOT applicable.** The actual time-domain response is $y(t) = 2 \sin(t)$, which oscillates and has no finite limit as $t \to \infty$. Applying FVT formally would give:

$$\lim_{s \to 0} sY(s) = 0$$

But this is **mathematically misleading** — the true $y(\infty)$ does not exist.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Tank Heater Input-Output Model

We consider a **continuously stirred tank heater (CSTH)** with the following configuration:

- Inlet volumetric flow rate $F$ (m³/s), inlet temperature $T_i$ (K)
- Tank: volume $V$ (m³), density $\rho$ (kg/m³), heat capacity $C_p$ (J/kg·K), temperature $T$ (K)
- Heat transfer through a jacket: heat transfer coefficient $U$ (W/m²·K), area $A$ (m²), jacket temperature $T_j$ (K)

### **Step 1 — Total Mass Balance (assume constant $\rho$)**

$$\rho \frac{dV}{dt} = \rho F_{in} - \rho F_{out}$$

For constant volume operation, $F_{in} = F_{out} = F$ (steady-state assumption), so $dV/dt = 0$.

### **Step 2 — Total Energy Balance (First Law for Open System)**

The rate of accumulation of internal energy in the tank equals the net energy inflow by mass plus the net heat input from the jacket:

$$\frac{d}{dt} \left( \rho V C_p T \right) = \rho F C_p T_i - \rho F C_p T + UA(T_j - T)$$

### **Step 3 — Substitute Constant $V$**

$$\rho V C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = \rho F C_p (T_i - T) + UA(T_j - T)$$

### **Step 4 — Expand the Right-Hand Side**

$$\rho V C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = \rho F C_p T_i - \rho F C_p T + UA T_j - UA T$$

### **Step 5 — Collect Terms in $T$ on the Left-Hand Side**

$$\rho V C_p \frac{dT}{dt} + (\rho F C_p + UA) T = \rho F C_p T_i + UA T_j$$

### **Step 6 — Write in Standard First-Order Form**

Divide throughout by the coefficient of $T$:

$$\frac{\rho V C_p}{\rho F C_p + UA} \cdot \frac{dT}{dt} + T = \frac{\rho F C_p}{\rho F C_p + UA} T_i + \frac{UA}{\rho F C_p + UA} T_j$$

### **Step 7 — Identify Parameters**

**Time constant:**

$$\tau = \frac{\rho V C_p}{\rho F C_p + UA}$$

**Steady-state gains:**

$$K_1 = \frac{\rho F C_p}{\rho F C_p + UA} \quad \text{(gain w.r.t. inlet temperature } T_i\text{)}$$

$$K_2 = \frac{UA}{\rho F C_p + UA} \quad \text{(gain w.r.t. jacket temperature } T_j\text{)}$$

The first-order ODE becomes:

$$\tau \frac{dT}{dt} + T = K_1 T_i + K_2 T_j$$

---

## 3.5 Solution of the Tank Heater Model

### **Case 1: Step Change in Inlet Temperature $T_i$ (with constant jacket)**

Let $T_j = T_{j,s} = $ constant, and $T_i(t) = T_{i,s} + \Delta T_i \cdot u(t)$, where $u(t)$ is the unit step.

**Take the Laplace transform** (zero initial conditions assumed for the deviation variables):

$$\tau s T'(s) + T'(s) = K_1 T_i'(s)$$

$$T'(s) (\tau s + 1) = K_1 \cdot \frac{\Delta T_i}{s}$$

$$T'(s) = \frac{K_1 \Delta T_i}{s(\tau s + 1)}$$

**Partial Fraction Decomposition:**

$$\frac{1}{s(\tau s + 1)} = \frac{A}{s} + \frac{B}{\tau s + 1}$$

Multiplying through: $1 = A(\tau s + 1) + Bs$

- Setting $s = 0$: $1 = A \Rightarrow A = 1$
- Setting $s = -1/\tau$: $1 = B(-1/\tau) \Rightarrow B = -\tau$

Therefore:

$$T'(s) = K_1 \Delta T_i \left[ \frac{1}{s} - \frac{\tau}{\tau s + 1} \right] = K_1 \Delta T_i \left[ \frac{1}{s} - \frac{1}{s + 1/\tau} \right]$$

**Inverse Laplace Transform:**

$$\boxed{T'(t) = K_1 \Delta T_i \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)}$$

### **Step 8 — Apply IVT and FVT to Verify**

**IVT check:**

$$T'(0^+) = \lim_{s \to \infty} s \cdot \frac{K_1 \Delta T_i}{s(\tau s + 1)} = \lim_{s \to \infty} \frac{K_1 \Delta T_i}{\tau s + 1} = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**FVT check** (poles of $sT'(s) = \frac{K_1 \Delta T_i}{\tau s + 1}$ at $s = -1/\tau$, in LHP):

$$T'(\infty) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{K_1 \Delta T_i}{s(\tau s + 1)} = \frac{K_1 \Delta T_i}{1} = K_1 \Delta T_i \quad \checkmark$$

> **Cross-validation:** The IVT result of $0$ means the tank temperature does not jump instantaneously (the liquid has thermal inertia). The FVT result of $K_1 \Delta T_i$ means the temperature eventually settles at a new steady state $K_1 \Delta T_i$ above the original.

---

### **Case 2: Numerical Worked Example**

A tank heater has the following data at steady state:

- $V = 2 \text{ m}^3$, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $C_p = 4186 \text{ J/kg·K}$
- $F_s = 0.01 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, $UA = 5000 \text{ W/K}$
- $T_{i,s} = 300 \text{ K}$, $T_s = 320 \text{ K}$ (steady-state tank temperature)

**Calculate $\tau$ and $K_1$:**

$$\tau = \frac{\rho V C_p}{\rho F C_p + UA} = \frac{1000 \cdot 2 \cdot 4186}{1000 \cdot 0.01 \cdot 4186 + 5000} = \frac{8.372 \times 10^6}{41860 + 5000} = \frac{8.372 \times 10^6}{46860}$$

$$\boxed{\tau \approx 178.7 \text{ s}}$$

$$K_1 = \frac{1000 \cdot 0.01 \cdot 4186}{46860} = \frac{41860}{46860} \approx 0.8934$$

**If $T_i$ is suddenly increased by $\Delta T_i = 10 \text{ K}$:**

**Response:**

$$T'(t) = 0.8934 \cdot 10 \cdot (1 - e^{-t/178.7}) = 8.934 (1 - e^{-t/178.7}) \text{ K}$$

- $T'(0^+) = 0$ K (no instantaneous jump, IVT confirmed).
- $T'(\infty) = 8.934$ K (new tank temperature = 320 + 8.934 = 328.93 K, FVT confirmed).
- $T'(\tau) = 8.934 (1 - e^{-1}) \approx 5.553$ K (63.2% of the final value reached at $t = \tau$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Physical Layout of the Stirred-Tank Heater

The schematic below captures the **mass and energy flow** within the control volume. Every arrow represents a transport phenomenon that contributes to the energy balance equation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inlet Stream: Flow F, Temperature Ti] -->|Mass and Energy Inflow| B((Stirred Tank: Volume V, Temperature T))
    J[Steam Jacket: Area A, Temperature Tj] -->|Heat Transfer Q = UA x Tj minus T| B
    B -->|Outlet Flow F, Temperature T| C[Process Outlet]
    B -.->|Accumulation: rho V Cp dT by dt| D[Internal Energy Storage]

    classDef input fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0C4A6E
    classDef tank fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:3px,color:#78350F
    classDef jacket fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,stroke-width:2px,color:#831843
    classDef outlet fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#065F46,stroke-width:2px,color:#064E3B
    classDef storage fill:#EDE9FE,stroke:#5B21B6,stroke-width:2px,color:#4C1D95

    class A input
    class B tank
    class J jacket
    class C outlet
    class D storage
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Input-Output Representation of the Tank Heater

This diagram treats the tank heater as a **transfer-function block** $G(s)$ with multiple input channels. This is the form in which KTU expects you to draw it for process control problems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Ti["Inlet Temperature Disturbance T_i (s)"] --> Sum1((+))
    Fj["Jacket Temperature Input T_j (s)"] --> Sum2((+))
    Sum1 --> Gain1["Gain K1 = Fs rho Cp divided by Fs rho Cp + UA"]
    Sum2 --> Gain2["Gain K2 = UA divided by Fs rho Cp + UA"]
    Gain1 --> Sum3((+))
    Gain2 --> Sum3
    Sum3 --> Dynamics["First-Order Dynamics 1 divided by tau s + 1, tau = V rho Cp divided by Fs rho Cp + UA"]
    Dynamics --> Output["Tank Temperature T (s)"]
    Output -.->|Feedback for closed-loop analysis| Sum1

    classDef input fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1D4ED8,stroke-width:2px,color:#1E3A8A
    classDef gain fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#78350F
    classDef dynamic fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,stroke-width:2px,color:#831843
    classDef output fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#065F46,stroke-width:2px,color:#064E3B
    classDef sum fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#B91C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#7F1D1D

    class Ti,Fj input
    class Gain1,Gain2 gain
    class Dynamics dynamic
    class Output output
    class Sum1,Sum2,Sum3 sum
```

## 4.3 Signal-Flow Graph of the Differential Equation

The energy balance ODE $\tau \dfrac{dT'}{dt} + T' = K_1 T_i' + K_2 T_j'$ can be visualized as an **integrator with feedback**:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In1["K1 T_i prime"] --> SumA((+))
    In2["K2 T_j prime"] --> SumA
    SumA --> Int["1 by s Integrator"]
    Int --> GainTau["1 by tau Gain"]
    GainTau --> OutputNode["T prime s"]
    OutputNode -->|Unity Feedback| SumFB((+))
    SumFB -->|Negative sign implicit| SumA

    classDef sig fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1D4ED8,stroke-width:2px,color:#1E3A8A
    classDef dyn fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,stroke-width:2px,color:#831843
    classDef out fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#065F46,stroke-width:2px,color:#064E3B
    classDef sum fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#B91C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#7F1D1D

    class In1,In2 sig
    class Int,GainTau dyn
    class OutputNode out
    class SumA,SumFB sum
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagrams for your KTU answer:**
> - The first diagram explains the *physical* system (what flows in/out).
> - The second diagram is the *control engineering* abstraction used to design feedback loops.
> - The third diagram is the *mathematical* state-space representation; the integrator block $\frac{1}{s}$ corresponds to the $\frac{dT'}{dt}$ term, and the unity feedback corresponds to the $-T'$ term on the LHS of the ODE.
