# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**

**Define "dead time" in a process control loop. Give two physical examples that produce dead time in chemical process plants.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Dead time** (or transportation lag) is a delay $\theta$ (in seconds) between an input change and the start of the corresponding output change in a process. Mathematically, if input changes at $t = 0$, the output begins to respond only at $t = \theta$. It is represented in the Laplace domain as $G(s) = e^{-\theta s}$.
>
> **Two physical examples:**
> 1. **Pipeline transportation lag** — A fluid flowing through a pipe of length $L$ at velocity $u$ introduces a dead time $\theta = L/u$ before a composition/temperature change at the upstream end reaches the downstream sensor.
> 2. **Analyzer cycle time** — A process gas chromatograph takes 3–5 minutes to draw a sample, separate the components, and report the composition, producing a measurement dead time.
>
> *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for pipeline example, 1 mark for analyzer example.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**

**Distinguish between the "inherent" and "installed" characteristics of a control valve. Why is the equal-percentage trim preferred for most process control applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> - **Inherent characteristic** is the relationship between flow $Q$ and stem position $h$ at a *constant* pressure drop across the valve (lab-tested, $\Delta P_{\text{valve}} = \Delta P_{\max}$).
> - **Installed characteristic** is the actual $Q$ vs $h$ relationship when the valve is *installed* in a piping system, where the valve pressure drop varies with flow (as line pressure drop increases, valve pressure drop decreases).
>
> **Why equal percentage is preferred:** When a linear-trim valve is installed in a system with significant line pressure drop, its installed characteristic becomes *non-linear* (gain drops at high flows), making controller tuning difficult. An **equal-percentage** trim has an *increasing* gain with stem travel, which exactly compensates for the gain reduction due to the piping system, yielding an approximately **linear installed characteristic** over a wide operating range. This gives uniform control quality and wide rangeability ($R = 25:1$ to $50:1$).
>
> *(1 mark for inherent definition, 1 mark for installed definition, 1 mark for equal-percentage justification.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Measuring Devices and Transmission Lines

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the transfer function of a **first-order measuring device** (sensor + transmitter) starting from the energy balance on a thermal element. Clearly state the assumptions. (7 marks)

**(b)** A pneumatic transmission line has tube length $L = 200$ m, tube radius $r = 3$ mm, air viscosity $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, line pressure $P = 25$ psig, and receiving volume $V_r = 50$ cm³. Compute (i) the pneumatic resistance $R$, (ii) the pneumatic capacitance $C$, (iii) the line time constant $\tau_L$, and (iv) the equivalent dead time $\theta_L$. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Sensor is a lumped thermal mass (Biot number < 0.1).
2. Heat-transfer coefficient $h$ and area $A$ are constant.
3. Perfect thermal contact between sensor and process.
4. No radial temperature gradient inside the sensor.

**Energy balance on the sensor:**

$$m c_p \frac{dT_m}{dt} = hA (T - T_m)$$

where $T$ = process temperature, $T_m$ = sensor temperature, $m c_p$ = thermal mass, $hA$ = conductance.

**Step 1 — Rearrange:**

$$\frac{m c_p}{hA} \frac{dT_m}{dt} + T_m = T$$

**Step 2 — Define measurement time constant:**

$$\tau_m = \frac{m c_p}{hA} \quad [\text{seconds}]$$

So the equation becomes:

$$\tau_m \frac{dT_m}{dt} + T_m = T$$

**Step 3 — Take the Laplace transform** (zero initial conditions):

$$\tau_m s T_m(s) + T_m(s) = T(s)$$

$$T_m(s) (\tau_m s + 1) = T(s)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the transfer function:**

$$\boxed{G_m(s) = \frac{T_m(s)}{T(s)} = \frac{1}{\tau_m s + 1}}$$

If the sensor has a static gain $K_m$ (transmitter span / process span), include it:

$$G_m(s) = \frac{K_m}{\tau_m s + 1}$$

**Step 5 — Step-response analysis (bonus, partial credit):**

For a unit-step input $T(s) = 1/s$:

$$T_m(t) = 1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}$$

- $T_m(\tau_m) = 0.632$ → 63.2% of final value
- $T_m(4\tau_m) = 0.982$ → 98.2% (settling time)
- $T_m(5\tau_m) = 0.993$ → 99.3% (practical settling time)

*(7 marks breakdown: 1 for assumptions, 2 for derivation, 2 for transfer function form, 1 for gain inclusion, 1 for step response.)*

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Given:**
- $L = 200$ m, $r = 3 \text{ mm} = 3 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s
- $P = 25$ psig = $25 \times 6894.76 = 1.72 \times 10^{5}$ Pa (gauge) → absolute $P_{\text{abs}} = 1.72 \times 10^{5} + 1.013 \times 10^{5} = 2.74 \times 10^{5}$ Pa
- $V_r = 50 \text{ cm}^3 = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$

**(i) Pneumatic resistance $R$ (Hagen–Poiseuille, laminar):**

$$R = \frac{8 \mu L}{\pi r^4}$$

**Substitute:**

$$R = \frac{8 \times (1.8 \times 10^{-5}) \times 200}{\pi \times (3 \times 10^{-3})^4}$$

**Numerator:** $8 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \times 200 = 2.88 \times 10^{-2}$

**Denominator:** $\pi \times 81 \times 10^{-12} = 2.545 \times 10^{-10}$

$$R = \frac{2.88 \times 10^{-2}}{2.545 \times 10^{-10}} = 1.13 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa·s/m}^3$$

**(ii) Pneumatic capacitance $C$:**

$$C = \frac{V_{\text{total}}}{P_{\text{abs}}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{2.74 \times 10^{5}} = 1.82 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}^3/\text{Pa}$$

(Note: We can include the tube volume $V_{\text{tube}} = \pi r^2 L = \pi \times 9 \times 10^{-6} \times 200 = 5.65 \times 10^{-3}$ m³, but for a control room with a small receiving instrument, the dominant capacitance is the receiving volume. If the question requires tube + receiver, $V_{\text{total}} = 5.65 \times 10^{-3} + 50 \times 10^{-6} \approx 5.70 \times 10^{-3}$ m³, giving $C \approx 2.08 \times 10^{-8}$ m³/Pa. Most textbook KTU problems use just the receiving volume.)

**(iii) Line time constant $\tau_L$:**

$$\tau_L = R \cdot C = 1.13 \times 10^{8} \times 1.82 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_L = 0.0206 \text{ s} \approx 20.6 \text{ ms}}$$

(If using the full tube volume: $\tau_L = 1.13 \times 10^{8} \times 2.08 \times 10^{-8} = 2.35$ s — the standard KTU answer for a 200 m line.)

**(iv) Equivalent dead time $\theta_L$:**

For a first-order system, the average delay (transportation lag equivalent) is $\theta_L \approx \tau_L / 2$:

$$\boxed{\theta_L \approx \frac{\tau_L}{2} \approx 1.18 \text{ s}}$$

(Using the full-volume answer: $\theta_L \approx 1.18$ s.)

**Transfer function of the line:**

$$G_L(s) = \frac{1}{\tau_L s + 1} = \frac{1}{2.35 s + 1} \quad \text{or} \quad G_L(s) \approx e^{-1.18 s}$$

*(7 marks breakdown: 2 for R, 1 for C, 2 for τL, 2 for θL.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Pneumatic Control Valves and Dead Time Processes

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **pneumatically actuated diaphragm valve with a positioner**. Derive its transfer function. (7 marks)

**(b)** A process has the transfer function:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{2 \, e^{-3s}}{5s + 1}$$

The control valve has transfer function $G_v(s) = \dfrac{0.5}{0.2s + 1}$ and the measurement has $G_m(s) = \dfrac{1}{1.5s + 1}$.

(i) Apply a **first-order Padé approximation** to the dead time and write the overall open-loop transfer function. (3 marks)
(ii) Determine the **closed-loop characteristic equation** for a unity-feedback system with proportional controller gain $K_c = 3$. (4 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Construction (with positioner):**

A pneumatic diaphragm-actuated control valve with a positioner consists of:

1. **Diaphragm actuator** — A flexible elastomer/diaphragm divides the actuator housing into upper (supply) and lower (vent) chambers. The controller air signal (3–15 psig) is fed to the lower chamber; the upper chamber is connected to a constant 20 psig supply.
2. **Spring** — A spring opposes the diaphragm force, providing a restoring force proportional to stem position.
3. **Valve stem and plug** — Connected to the diaphragm, moves linearly or rotationally to throttle the flow.
4. **Positioner** — A local feedback controller that compares actual stem position $h$ (via a mechanical link) with the set pressure $P_c$ and bleeds air into or vents air from the actuator to drive $h$ to its correct value. This is a **local closed loop** inside the valve.

**Working Principle:**

When $P_c$ increases, the diaphragm deflects down, compressing the spring and moving the stem down. The plug moves toward the seat, reducing flow. The positioner continuously adjusts actuator pressure to make $h$ linearly track $P_c$, eliminating the effect of stem friction and process pressure fluctuations on the stem.

**Transfer Function Derivation:**

Force balance on the diaphragm-stem-plug assembly (with $m \to 0$ for diaphragm actuators, dominant friction $f$):

$$f \frac{dh}{dt} + k h = A (P_c - P_0)$$

where $A$ = diaphragm area, $k$ = spring constant, $P_0$ = reference pressure.

**Step 1:** Rearrange:

$$f \frac{dh}{dt} = A (P_c - P_0) - k h$$

**Step 2:** Linearize around operating point. Define deviation stem position $\bar{h}$ and deviation pressure $\bar{P_c}$:

$$f \frac{d\bar{h}}{dt} + k \bar{h} = A \bar{P_c}$$

**Step 3:** Divide by $k$:

$$\frac{f}{k} \frac{d\bar{h}}{dt} + \bar{h} = \frac{A}{k} \bar{P_c}$$

**Step 4:** Define valve parameters: $K_v = A/k$ (gain) and $\tau_v = f/k$ (time constant).

$$\tau_v \frac{d\bar{h}}{dt} + \bar{h} = K_v \bar{P_c}$$

**Step 5:** Take Laplace transform:

$$\tau_v s H(s) + H(s) = K_v P_c(s)$$

$$H(s) (\tau_v s + 1) = K_v P_c(s)$$

**Step 6 — Transfer function from $P_c$ to $h$:**

$$\frac{H(s)}{P_c(s)} = \frac{K_v}{\tau_v s + 1}$$

**Step 7 — Convert stem position to flow:**

If the valve trim has gain $K_{Qv}$ (linearized flow coefficient near operating point):

$$Q(s) = K_{Qv} H(s)$$

**Step 8 — Overall valve transfer function:**

$$\boxed{G_v(s) = \frac{Q(s)}{P_c(s)} = \frac{K_v K_{Qv}}{\tau_v s + 1} = \frac{K_v^*}{\tau_v s + 1}}$$

where $K_v^* = K_v K_{Qv}$ is the **overall valve gain** (in flow units / pressure units, e.g., m³/h per psig).

*(7 marks: 2 for construction diagram/description, 2 for working principle, 3 for transfer function derivation.)*

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Given:**
$$G_p(s) = \frac{2 e^{-3s}}{5s + 1}, \quad G_v(s) = \frac{0.5}{0.2s + 1}, \quad G_m(s) = \frac{1}{1.5s + 1}, \quad K_c = 3$$

**Step 1 — Apply first-order Padé approximation to $e^{-3s}$:**

$$e^{-3s} \approx \frac{1 - \frac{3s}{2}}{1 + \frac{3s}{2}} = \frac{1 - 1.5s}{1 + 1.5s}$$

**Step 2 — Process transfer function with Padé:**

$$G_p(s) \approx \frac{2(1 - 1.5s)}{(5s + 1)(1 + 1.5s)} = \frac{2 - 3s}{(5s + 1)(1 + 1.5s)}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply with valve and measurement:**

$$G_{OL}(s) = K_c \cdot G_p(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_m(s)$$

$$= 3 \times \frac{2 - 3s}{(5s + 1)(1 + 1.5s)} \times \frac{0.5}{0.2s + 1} \times \frac{1}{1.5s + 1}$$

**Combine constants:** $3 \times 2 \times 0.5 \times 1 = 3$

$$G_{OL}(s) = \frac{3(1 - 1.5s)}{(5s + 1)(1 + 1.5s)(0.2s + 1)(1.5s + 1)}$$

**Step 4 — Closed-loop characteristic equation:**

For unity feedback, the characteristic equation is:

$$1 + G_{OL}(s) = 0$$

$$(5s + 1)(1 + 1.5s)(0.2s + 1)(1.5s + 1) + 3(1 - 1.5s) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Expand the denominator product:**

$(5s + 1)(1.5s + 1) = 7.5s^2 + 5s + 1.5s + 1 = 7.5s^2 + 6.5s + 1$

$(0.2s + 1)(1 + 1.5s) = 0.2s + 0.3s^2 + 1 + 1.5s = 0.3s^2 + 1.7s + 1$

**Product of products:**

$(7.5s^2 + 6.5s + 1)(0.3s^2 + 1.7s + 1)$

Let $A = 7.5s^2 + 6.5s + 1$ and $B = 0.3s^2 + 1.7s + 1$:

$A \times B$:

- $s^4$ term: $7.5 \times 0.3 = 2.25$
- $s^3$ term: $7.5 \times 1.7 + 6.5 \times 0.3 = 12.75 + 1.95 = 14.7$
- $s^2$ term: $7.5 \times 1 + 6.5 \times 1.7 + 1 \times 0.3 = 7.5 + 11.05 + 0.3 = 18.85$
- $s^1$ term: $6.5 \times 1 + 1 \times 1.7 = 6.5 + 1.7 = 8.2$
- $s^0$ term: $1 \times 1 = 1$

So $A \times B = 2.25 s^4 + 14.7 s^3 + 18.85 s^2 + 8.2 s + 1$

**Step 6 — Add the Padé numerator term $3(1 - 1.5s) = -4.5s + 3$:**

$$\boxed{2.25 s^4 + 14.7 s^3 + 18.85 s^2 + 8.2 s + 1 + (-4.5 s + 3) = 0}$$

$$\boxed{2.25 s^4 + 14.7 s^3 + 18.85 s^2 + 3.7 s + 4 = 0}$$

**Step 7 — Apply Routh–Hurwitz (bonus for stability insight):**

| $s^4$ | 2.25 | 18.85 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | 14.7 | 3.7 | 0 |
| $s^2$ | $\frac{14.7 \times 18.85 - 2.25 \times 3.7}{14.7} = \frac{277.1 - 8.325}{14.7} = 18.27$ | 4 | |
| $s^1$ | $\frac{18.27 \times 3.7 - 14.7 \times 4}{18.27} = \frac{67.6 - 58.8}{18.27} = 0.481$ | | |
| $s^0$ | 4 | | |

All first-column entries are **positive** → system is **stable** for $K_c = 3$.

*(7 marks: 2 for Padé, 2 for OL assembly, 3 for characteristic equation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up deviation variables with absolute values** — Always use deviation form (e.g., $\bar{T} = T - T_0$) before linearizing. Students who plug absolute values into Laplace transforms lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply the Padé approximation** — A dead time $e^{-\theta s}$ in the characteristic equation makes it transcendental and unsolvable by Routh. Always apply Padé first.
> 3. **Wrong sign in Padé numerator** — The 1st-order Padé is $(1 - \theta s/2)/(1 + \theta s/2)$. Students often write it the other way around. Verify by checking $s = 0$ gives $1$.
> 4. **Span vs. absolute units in transmitter gain** — $K_m$ is **deviation out / deviation in** (e.g., mA/°C, or psi/°C), not the absolute transmitted signal.
> 5. **Equal percentage valve constant $\beta$ vs. rangeability $R$** — $\beta = \ln R$. Mixing them up is a very common error.
> 6. **Treating $\theta_L = L/v_s$ as exact** — This is for ideal incompressible fluid. For air in a long tube, the "average delay" of a first-order system is $\tau_L/2$, not $L/v_s$.
> 7. **Not stating units in the final answer** — KTU awards ½ mark for correct units; don't lose it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Measuring device $G_m(s)$** is *first-order* ($K_m/(\tau_m s + 1)$) for most sensors; second-order or inverse-response are exceptions.
- [x] **Transmission line $G_L(s)$** is *first-order* ($1/(\tau_L s + 1)$) for short pneumatic lines; *pure dead time* ($e^{-\theta s}$) for long lines; *pure gain* for electronic lines.
- [x] **Pneumatic valve (with positioner)**: $G_v(s) = K_v/(\tau_v s + 1)$.
- [x] **Pneumatic valve (friction-dominated, no positioner)**: $G_v(s) = K_v e^{-\theta_v s}$ (dead band → limit cycles).
- [x] **Valve inherent characteristics**:
  - Linear: $Q = Q_{\max} h$
  - Equal percentage: $Q/Q_{\max} = R^{h-1}$, gain = $Q \ln R$
  - Quick opening: $Q = Q_{\max}\sqrt{h}$ (or linear with on/off trim)
- [x] **Equal percentage is preferred** in process control because its *installed* characteristic is approximately linear over a wide operating range.
- [x] **Rangeability $R$** = $Q_{\max}/Q_{\min}$ at the operating pressure drop; typical $R = 25$ to $50$ for equal-percentage.
- [x] **Dead time** $G(s) = e^{-\theta s}$ arises from material transport, analyzer cycle time, and communication delay.
- [x] **1st-order Padé**: $e^{-\theta s} \approx (1 - \theta s/2)/(1 + \theta s/2)$.
- [x] **2nd-order Padé**: $e^{-\theta s} \approx (1 - \theta s/2 + \theta^2 s^2/12)/(1 + \theta s/2 + \theta^2 s^2/12)$.
- [x] **FOPDT model**: $G_p(s) = K_p e^{-\theta s}/(\tau_p s + 1)$ is the workhorse of process control tuning.
- [x] **Standard signals**: 3–15 psig (pneumatic), 4–20 mA (electronic), 20 psig (pneumatic supply).
- [x] **Pneumatic line parameters**: $R = 8\mu L/(\pi r^4)$ (laminar) and $C = V_{\text{total}}/P_{\text{abs}}$.
- [x] **Time constant of a first-order system**: $T(\tau) = 0.632 \cdot T_{\text{final}}$ (63.2% rule).
- [x] **Settling time** of a first-order system: $\approx 4\tau$ (within 2%) or $5\tau$ (within 0.7%).
- [x] **Phase lag at $\omega = 1/\tau$** for a first-order system: $-45°$.
- [x] **Phase lag at $\omega \theta = 1$ rad** for pure dead time: $-57.3°$ (i.e., $-\omega\theta$ in radians).
- [x] **Routh–Hurwitz** can be applied to the closed-loop characteristic equation **only after** replacing $e^{-\theta s}$ with a Padé rational approximation.
- [x] **Boiler drum level** is the classic *inverse-response* example (right-half-plane zero).
- [x] **Long pneumatic lines (>100 m)** must be modelled as pure dead time, not first-order lag.
- [x] **Diaphragm actuator mass is negligible** — the dynamics are first-order, not second-order, when friction dominates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The single most important takeaway for KTU Module 3:**
> Every hardware component of a feedback loop can be represented as a transfer function of the form $K/(\tau s + 1)$, $e^{-\theta s}$, or a combination. The **dead time** is the most insidious of these because it adds phase lag without amplitude reduction, severely limiting the achievable controller gain. The **equal-percentage valve characteristic** is the *only* valve trim that gives approximately linear installed behaviour, which is why it is the industry default for throttling service.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of feedback controllers; Control laws and transfer functions of P, PI, and PID controllers; Dynamic behaviour of feedback-controlled processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT601 - PROCESS DYNAMICS CONTROL & INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of feedback controllers; Control laws and transfer functions of P, PI, and PID controllers; Dynamic behaviour of feedback-controlled processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Types of Feedback Controllers

A **feedback controller** is a device or software algorithm that automatically adjusts a manipulated variable (MV) based on the error signal — the difference between the desired setpoint (SP) and the measured process variable (PV) — to maintain the process at its desired operating condition without continuous human intervention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> A feedback controller compares the measured output of a process with the desired setpoint, calculates the error, and generates a corrective control action that drives the process back to the setpoint. The action is **closed-loop** because the output is continuously fed back to the input.

### Classification of Feedback Controllers

Feedback controllers are broadly classified based on their **control law** (mathematical relationship between error and controller output):

| **Controller Type** | **Control Action** | **Mathematical Form** |
|---|---|---|
| **P (Proportional)** | Output proportional to current error | $u(t) = K_c \, e(t)$ |
| **I (Integral)** | Output proportional to integral of error | $u(t) = \dfrac{K_c}{\tau_I} \int e(t)\, dt$ |
| **D (Derivative)** | Output proportional to rate of change of error | $u(t) = K_c \, \tau_D \, \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ |
| **PI** | Proportional + Integral | $u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I}\int e(t)\, dt \right]$ |
| **PD** | Proportional + Derivative | $u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \tau_D \dfrac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$ |
| **PID** | Proportional + Integral + Derivative | $u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I}\int e(t)\, dt + \tau_D \dfrac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$ |

Other classifications based on **hardware/structure** include:
- **Electronic controllers** (op-amp based, industrial DCS/PLC based)
- **Pneumatic controllers** (3-15 psi signal, used in older plants)
- **Hydraulic controllers** (high-power actuation)
- **Digital/Software controllers** (microprocessor/DCS based)
- **Single-loop vs. Multi-loop (cascade) controllers**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Think of It Like Driving a Car

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine you are driving a car and want to maintain a constant speed of **60 km/h** on a highway. Your eyes continuously look at the speedometer (PV), your brain compares it with 60 (SP), and your foot adjusts the accelerator.
>
> - **P (Proportional):** The harder the error (difference from 60), the more you press/release the accelerator. If you are at 50, you press harder; if at 58, you press gently. But you'll always settle slightly below 60 — this is **offset**.
> - **I (Integral):** You remember past mistakes. If you've been at 58 for 3 minutes, you gradually press harder and harder until you reach exactly 60. This **eliminates offset**.
> - **D (Derivative):** You anticipate the future. If you see the road sloping downhill, you ease off the accelerator *before* your speed actually increases. This **anticipates change** and dampens oscillations.

This is precisely how industrial controllers work on temperature, pressure, flow, and level loops in chemical plants!

---

## 1.3 Dynamic Behaviour of Feedback-Controlled Processes

The **dynamic behaviour** of a feedback-controlled process describes how the closed-loop system (process + controller + sensor + final control element) responds over time when subjected to disturbances or setpoint changes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Metrics for Feedback-Controlled Processes:**
> - **Offset (Steady-State Error):** The residual error remaining after a long time.
> - **Maximum Overshoot (%OS):** Peak excursion beyond the setpoint.
> - **Rise Time ($t_r$):** Time to first reach the setpoint.
> - **Settling Time ($t_s$):** Time to remain within ±5% (or ±2%) of the setpoint.
> - **Decay Ratio (DR):** Ratio of successive peak deviations.
> - **Period of Oscillation ($P$):** Time between successive peaks.
> - **Stability:** Whether oscillations decay or grow unbounded.

These metrics are what every KTU examiner expects you to draw, label, and discuss on a typical disturbance response graph.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a Feedback Control Loop

A standard closed-loop control system consists of four essential elements:

1. **Process (Plant):** The system whose output (PV) is to be controlled (e.g., a heat exchanger, CSTR, distillation column).
2. **Measurement / Sensor (Transmitter):** Measures the PV and converts it to a standardized signal (e.g., 4–20 mA).
3. **Controller:** Compares PV with SP, computes error, applies a control law, and outputs a controller signal.
4. **Final Control Element (Actuator / Valve):** Implements the corrective action by adjusting a manipulated variable (e.g., control valve varying steam flow).

The **error signal** is defined as:

$$e(t) = Y_{sp}(t) - Y_m(t)$$

where $Y_{sp}$ is the setpoint and $Y_m$ is the measured PV.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the negative sign matters:**
> In Laplace domain feedback analysis, the standard convention is $E(s) = Y_{sp}(s) - Y_m(s)$. The closed-loop transfer function always has the form $\dfrac{G_c G_p G_v}{1 + G_c G_p G_v G_m}$ where the negative feedback introduces the characteristic "1 +" in the denominator — the foundation of stability analysis.

---

## 2.2 Control Laws and Transfer Functions

### 2.2.1 Proportional (P) Controller

**Control Law (Time Domain):**
$$u(t) = u_{ss} + K_c \, e(t)$$

**Transfer Function:**
$$\frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = G_c(s) = K_c$$

**Key Characteristics:**
- $K_c$ is the **proportional gain** (dimensionless, but controller-dependent units).
- High $K_c$ → faster response but more oscillation; can lead to instability.
- Produces a **steady-state offset** for a step change (cannot eliminate it).

### 2.2.2 Proportional-Integral (PI) Controller

**Control Law (Time Domain):**
$$u(t) = u_{ss} + K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int_0^t e(t')\, dt' \right]$$

**Transfer Function:**
$$G_c(s) = K_c \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s} \right] = K_c \left[ \frac{\tau_I s + 1}{\tau_I s} \right]$$

**Key Characteristics:**
- $\tau_I$ = **integral time (reset time)** in minutes/seconds. Smaller $\tau_I$ → stronger integral action.
- **Eliminates offset** in most cases.
- Increases order of the system, may slow down response or cause oscillations if $\tau_I$ is too small.
- Most widely used controller in process industries (~80% of loops).

### 2.2.3 Proportional-Derivative (PD) Controller

**Control Law (Time Domain):**
$$u(t) = u_{ss} + K_c \left[ e(t) + \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

**Transfer Function:**
$$G_c(s) = K_c \left[ 1 + \tau_D s \right]$$

**Key Characteristics:**
- $\tau_D$ = **derivative time**.
- Provides **anticipatory action** based on rate of change of error.
- Improves stability, reduces overshoot, but **does not eliminate offset**.
- Sensitive to measurement noise (a real KTU exam pitfall!).

### 2.2.4 Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) Controller

**Control Law (Time Domain):**
$$u(t) = u_{ss} + K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int_0^t e(t')\, dt' + \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

**Transfer Function (Ideal/Parallel Form):**
$$G_c(s) = K_c \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s \right] = K_c \left[ \frac{\tau_I \tau_D s^2 + \tau_I s + 1}{\tau_I s} \right]$$

**Transfer Function (Standard/ISA Form — also frequently asked):**
$$G_c(s) = K_c \left[ \frac{\tau_I s + 1}{\tau_I s} \right] \left[ \tau_D s + 1 \right]$$

**Key Characteristics:**
- Combines benefits of P, I, and D actions.
- $K_c$, $\tau_I$, $\tau_D$ are the **three tuning parameters**.
- Gives best dynamic performance when properly tuned.
- Three commercial forms exist: **Ideal (Parallel)**, **Series (Cascade)**, and **Standard (ISA)**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 Board-Exam relevant. Memorize the table.**

| **Controller** | **Control Law $u(t)$** | **Transfer Function $G_c(s)$** | **Key Effect** |
|---|---|---|---|
| P | $K_c\, e(t)$ | $K_c$ | Reduces rise time, causes offset |
| I | $\dfrac{K_c}{\tau_I} \int e(t)\, dt$ | $\dfrac{K_c}{\tau_I s}$ | Eliminates offset, slow |
| D | $K_c \tau_D \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | $K_c \tau_D s$ | Anticipates, reduces overshoot, noisy |
| PI | $K_c \left[ e(t) + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I}\int e\, dt \right]$ | $K_c \left( 1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s} \right)$ | Eliminates offset, industry standard |
| PD | $K_c \left[ e(t) + \tau_D \dfrac{de}{dt} \right]$ | $K_c (1 + \tau_D s)$ | Faster, no offset elimination |
| PID | $K_c \left[ e + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I}\int e\, dt + \tau_D \dfrac{de}{dt} \right]$ | $K_c \left( 1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s \right)$ | Best of all, three tuning knobs |

**Closed-Loop Transfer Function (General):**
$$\frac{Y(s)}{Y_{sp}(s)} = \frac{G_c(s)\, G_p(s)\, G_v(s)}{1 + G_c(s)\, G_p(s)\, G_v(s)\, G_m(s)}$$

**Offset Calculation (P-only, step disturbance, steady-state):**
$$E_{ss} = \frac{D_0}{1 + K_c\, K_p\, K_v\, K_m}$$

where $D_0$ is the disturbance magnitude. Notice: offset is **never zero** for finite $K_c$.

**Offset Calculation (PI controller, same case):**
$$E_{ss} = 0$$

because integral action drives the integral of error to zero.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a **refinery distillation column**, the PI controller is used to control tray temperature by manipulating steam flow to the reboiler. The P-action gives fast initial response, while the I-action ensures the temperature **exactly** matches the setpoint despite feed composition disturbances. PD-action is added when controlling **batch reactors** where temperature overshoots must be prevented to avoid runaway exothermic reactions (a critical safety concern in chemical plants). The **derivative filter** (often a first-order lag with time constant $\tau_D / N$, where $N = 10$) is added in real implementation to prevent noise amplification — a frequent 7-mark question in KTU exams.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: P-Controller and the Origin of Offset

Consider a first-order process with transfer function:
$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$$

A unity-feedback P-control loop with controller $G_c(s) = K_c$ has closed-loop transfer function:

$$\frac{Y(s)}{Y_{sp}(s)} = \frac{K_c G_p(s)}{1 + K_c G_p(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{K_c K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}}{1 + \dfrac{K_c K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}}$$

Simplifying:

$$\frac{Y(s)}{Y_{sp}(s)} = \frac{K_c K_p}{\tau_p s + 1 + K_c K_p}$$

For a unit step setpoint change $Y_{sp}(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$:

$$Y(s) = \frac{K_c K_p}{s(\tau_p s + 1 + K_c K_p)}$$

Applying the Final Value Theorem:

$$y_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s\, Y(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{K_c K_p}{\tau_p s + 1 + K_c K_p} = \frac{K_c K_p}{1 + K_c K_p}$$

Therefore the **steady-state offset** is:

$$e_{ss} = 1 - y_{ss} = 1 - \frac{K_c K_p}{1 + K_c K_p} = \frac{1}{1 + K_c K_p}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** As $K_c \to \infty$, $e_{ss} \to 0$ but the system becomes unstable in practice. The P-controller can never truly eliminate offset for finite $K_c$ — this is the most common 7-mark question in KTU exams.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: PI-Controller Eliminates Offset

With a PI controller $G_c(s) = K_c \left( 1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s} \right) = \dfrac{K_c (\tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I s}$, and unit feedback, the closed-loop transfer function becomes:

$$\frac{Y(s)}{Y_{sp}(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{K_c (\tau_I s + 1) K_p}{\tau_I s (\tau_p s + 1)}}{1 + \dfrac{K_c (\tau_I s + 1) K_p}{\tau_I s (\tau_p s + 1)}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $\tau_I s (\tau_p s + 1)$:

$$\frac{Y(s)}{Y_{sp}(s)} = \frac{K_c K_p (\tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I s (\tau_p s + 1) + K_c K_p (\tau_I s + 1)}$$

Expanding the denominator:

$$\tau_I \tau_p s^2 + \tau_I s + K_c K_p \tau_I s + K_c K_p = \tau_I \tau_p s^2 + \tau_I (1 + K_c K_p) s + K_c K_p$$

For a unit step:

$$y_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{K_c K_p (\tau_I s + 1)}{s[\tau_I \tau_p s^2 + \tau_I (1 + K_c K_p) s + K_c K_p]}$$

$$y_{ss} = \frac{K_c K_p \cdot 1}{K_c K_p} = 1$$

Therefore: **$e_{ss} = 1 - 1 = 0$** → Offset is **eliminated**. This is the *raison d'être* of the integral term.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Effect of Each Controller Term on Closed-Loop Poles

Take a first-order process with PI control. The characteristic equation is:

$$1 + G_c(s) G_p(s) = 0$$

$$1 + \frac{K_c K_p (\tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I s (\tau_p s + 1)} = 0$$

Multiplying through by $\tau_I s (\tau_p s + 1)$:

$$\tau_I \tau_p s^2 + \tau_I s + K_c K_p (\tau_I s + 1) = 0$$

$$\tau_I \tau_p s^2 + [\tau_I + K_c K_p \tau_I] s + K_c K_p = 0$$

Dividing by $\tau_I \tau_p$:

$$s^2 + \frac{1 + K_c K_p}{\tau_p} s + \frac{K_c K_p}{\tau_I \tau_p} = 0$$

Comparing with the standard second-order form $s^2 + 2\zeta \omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K_c K_p}{\tau_I \tau_p}}$$

$$\zeta = \frac{1 + K_c K_p}{2} \sqrt{\frac{\tau_I}{K_c K_p \tau_p}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Conclusions:**
> - Increasing $K_c$ → increases $\omega_n$ (faster response) but **reduces $\zeta$** (more oscillatory).
> - Increasing $\tau_I$ → reduces $\omega_n$ (slower) and **increases $\zeta$** (less oscillatory).
> - This is the theoretical foundation of **Ziegler-Nichols tuning** — the KTU's most-frequently-asked tuning method.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: P, PI, PID Performance Summary

| **Property** | **P** | **PI** | **PD** | **PID** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Steady-State Offset | ✗ Present | ✓ Zero | ✗ Present | ✓ Zero |
| Speed of Response | Fast | Moderate | Fast | Fast |
| Overshoot | High (if $K_c$ large) | Moderate | Low | Low (well-tuned) |
| Noise Sensitivity | Low | Low | **High** | High |
| Stability Margin | Lowest | Moderate | Higher | Highest |
| Number of Tuning Knobs | 1 | 2 | 2 | 3 |
| Industrial Use | Rare | **Most common** | Flow loops | Temperature, composition |

---

## 3.5 Closed-Loop Response Shapes — The 4 Standard Forms

When a feedback-controlled process is subjected to a step change in setpoint or disturbance, the dynamic response takes one of four standard shapes — these are the **figures examiners LOVE to ask you to draw and label**.

| **Response Type** | **Characteristics** | **Typical Controller** |
|---|---|---|
| **Overdamped (No Oscillation)** | Smooth approach, no overshoot, slow | High $K_c$ P-only on high-$\tau_p$ process |
| **Underdamped (Oscillatory)** | Decaying oscillations, overshoot, faster | P, PI, or PID with moderate $K_c$ |
| **Critically Damped** | Fastest response with no overshoot | Tightly tuned PI |
| **Sustained Oscillation (Marginal)** | Constant-amplitude oscillation | $K_c$ at **ultimate gain $K_{cu}$** |
| **Unstable** | Growing oscillations | $K_c > K_{cu}$ — loop fails |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to draw and label the **decay ratio (DR = 1/4)** which is the target for Ziegler-Nichols tuning. Examiners allocate **1–2 marks** purely for correct labelling of $t_r$, $t_p$, $t_s$, %OS, and DR on a graph.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Feedback Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Setpoint SP] -->|r sub t| B[Summing Junction]
    B -->|error e sub t| C[Controller Gc of s]
    C -->|u sub t| D[Final Control Element Gv of s]
    D -->|manipulated variable| E[Process Gp of s]
    E -->|output y sub t| F[Measurement Gm of s]
    F -->|measured PV ym sub t| B
    E -.->|disturbance d sub t| D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> The signal flows in a **closed loop** — the output is fed back to the summing junction (often denoted with a "−" for negative feedback) where it is subtracted from the setpoint. The **disturbance** enters additively at the process input in the most common representation, although it can also enter at the output depending on the textbook convention.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — PI Controller Internal Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Error e sub t] --> B[Summing Junction Internal]
    B --> C[P term: multiply by Kc]
    C --> D[Sum Block]
    A --> E[I term: integrate then multiply by Kc by tauI]
    E --> D
    D --> F[Controller Output u sub t]
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow — Closed-Loop Dynamic Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Step input in Setpoint or Disturbance]) --> Step1[Process receives new input]
    Step1 --> Step2[Process output y sub t begins to deviate]
    Step2 --> Step3[Sensor measures ym sub t]
    Step3 --> Step4[Controller computes error e = SP - ym]
    Step4 --> Step5{Controller Action}
    Step5 -->|P action| P6[Proportional correction Kc times e]
    Step5 -->|I action| I6[Integral correction removes offset]
    Step5 -->|D action| D6[Derivative correction anticipates trend]
    P6 --> Combine[Sum P, I, D contributions]
    I6 --> Combine
    D6 --> Combine
    Combine --> Step7[Final control element adjusts MV]
    Step7 --> Step8[New process response observed]
    Step8 --> Check{Error still present and t less than settling time}
    Check -->|Yes| Step3
    Check -->|No| End([System reaches steady state])
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic — Typical Closed-Loop Response with Annotations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The figure below is a textual rendering of the canonical step-response graph every KTU student must be able to reproduce. A hand-drawn version with axis labels and markings is the expected answer format.**

```
         y(t)
          ↑
          |              _  Peak 1 (overshoot)
          |            /   \_
          |           /       \_         Peak 2
          |          /          \       _/
          |         /            \     /
          |        /              \   /
          |       /                \_/        ← SP (setpoint)
          |      /                           
          |     /                       _____________  ← final value
          |    /                  _____/
          |   /             _____/
          |  /         _____/
          | /    _____/
          |/____/________________________________→ t
          |     ↑    ↑      ↑        ↑
          |   t_d   t_r    t_p      t_s
          |
```

**Annotation legend:**
- $t_d$ = dead time (delay)
- $t_r$ = rise time
- $t_p$ = time to first peak
- $t_s$ = settling time
- %OS = $\dfrac{\text{Peak 1} - \text{SP}}{\text{SP}} \times 100\%$
- Decay Ratio (DR) = $\dfrac{\text{Peak 2 deviation from SP}}{\text{Peak 1 deviation from SP}}$ (target: 1/4 for Z-N tuning)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The shaded region under the curve before the first peak crossing is often used in questions asking for **Integral Absolute Error (IAE)** or **Integral Square Error (ISE)** — both are common performance indices worth 2–3 marks in Part B questions.
