topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT601 - PROCESS DYNAMICS CONTROL & INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Prediction of stability of transfer function for open loop and closed loop systems; Characteristic equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Stability — The Foundation of Control Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **linear time-invariant (LTI) system** is said to be **stable** if and only if every bounded input produces a bounded output. This is the **Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO)** stability criterion. In the Laplace domain, this translates to a strict requirement on the location of the **poles** of the system's transfer function.

**Stability in the s-plane (poles only — zeros do NOT affect stability):**

| System Type | Stability Condition | Location of Poles |
|---|---|---|
| **Stable** | All poles lie in the **left-half of s-plane (LHP)** | Re(s) < 0 for every pole |
| **Marginally Stable** | Simple (non-repeated) poles lie on the imaginary axis, rest in LHP | Re(s) = 0 for some simple poles |
| **Unstable** | At least one pole in the **right-half of s-plane (RHP)** or repeated poles on jω-axis | Re(s) > 0 for at least one pole |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why poles and not zeros?** Because the time-domain response is a sum of exponential terms of the form $e^{p_i t}$ where $p_i$ are poles. The system's transient behaviour is governed entirely by the **denominator** of the transfer function. Zeros only affect the *shape* of the response (residues), not its boundedness.

---

## 1.2 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop — Conceptual Analogy

### Intuition with a Real-World Example
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Open Door vs The Spring-Loaded Door:**
> - **Open-loop system** is like a door without a closer — if you push it, it keeps swinging. Without external correction, any disturbance can cause the output to drift to infinity (unstable) or die out (stable), but you have **no feedback** to *enforce* stability.
> - **Closed-loop system** is like a door with a hydraulic spring. It constantly senses its own angle (feedback) and adjusts the damping force. **Stability now depends on the loop gain and dynamics**, not just the original door dynamics.

### Key Distinction
- **Open-loop stability** is a property of the plant/process alone.
- **Closed-loop stability** depends on the plant **AND** the controller **AND** the feedback path simultaneously.

---

## 1.3 The Characteristic Equation — The Heart of Closed-Loop Stability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> The **characteristic equation** of a closed-loop control system is the denominator polynomial of the closed-loop transfer function set equal to **zero**. It is mathematically expressed as:
>
> $$\boxed{1 + G(s)H(s) = 0}$$
>
> where $G(s)$ is the forward path transfer function and $H(s)$ is the feedback path transfer function.

The **roots of the characteristic equation are the closed-loop poles** — they determine whether the closed-loop system is stable, marginally stable, or unstable.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic is High-Yield for KTU

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:**
> KTU board papers (2024 scheme) frequently test:
> 1. Derivation of the closed-loop transfer function and characteristic equation.
> 2. Identification of open-loop poles vs closed-loop poles.
> 3. Direct application of stability rules using pole locations.
> 4. Setting up characteristic equations for cascade/parallel systems.
>
> Marks are routinely lost when students confuse the **denominator of G(s)H(s)** (open-loop poles) with the **closed-loop characteristic equation**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Open-Loop Transfer Function — Stability Prediction

### 2.1.1 Open-Loop Transfer Function
Consider a plant/process described by a transfer function:

$$G_{OL}(s) = \frac{N(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{b_m s^m + b_{m-1} s^{m-1} + \cdots + b_1 s + b_0}{a_n s^n + a_{n-1} s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_1 s + a_0}$$

where:
- $N(s)$ = numerator polynomial
- $D(s)$ = denominator polynomial
- The **poles of the open-loop system** are the roots of $D(s) = 0$.

### 2.1.2 Stability Rule for Open-Loop Systems
> [!IMPORTANT]
> An open-loop system is **stable** if and only if **every pole of $G_{OL}(s)$ lies strictly in the left-half s-plane**. The open-loop characteristic equation is simply $D(s) = 0$.

### 2.1.3 Why Open-Loop Stability is Not Enough
Even if a plant is open-loop stable, connecting it in a feedback loop can **destabilize** it. Conversely, an open-loop unstable plant can sometimes be made closed-loop stable with the right controller. This is precisely why the **characteristic equation** matters.

---

## 2.2 Closed-Loop Transfer Function — Full Derivation Setup

Consider the **standard unity negative feedback** configuration:

**Forward path:** $G(s)$
**Feedback path:** $H(s)$

The closed-loop transfer function is derived from:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The closed-loop transfer function can also be written compactly as:
>
> $$\boxed{T(s) = \frac{G(s)H(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)} \cdot \frac{1}{H(s)}}$$
>
> The unity feedback case is obtained when $H(s) = 1$, simplifying to $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$.

---

## 2.3 The Characteristic Equation — Detailed Construction

### 2.3.1 General Form
The **closed-loop characteristic equation** is the denominator of $T(s)$ set to zero:

$$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$$

If we substitute $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{N_p(s)}{D_p(s)}$, we get:

$$1 + \frac{N_p(s)}{D_p(s)} = 0$$

Multiplying throughout by $D_p(s)$:

$$\boxed{D_p(s) + N_p(s) = 0}$$

This is the **expanded characteristic equation** in polynomial form.

### 2.3.2 Order of the Characteristic Equation
> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **order of the characteristic equation = order of the closed-loop system = number of closed-loop poles = maximum of (order of G, order of H) + 0**, but in general it equals the order of $G(s)H(s)$ when simplified, *unless* pole-zero cancellation occurs at the $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ boundary.

### 2.3.3 Relationship Between Open-Loop and Closed-Loop Poles

| Property | Open-Loop Poles | Closed-Loop Poles |
|---|---|---|
| **Where they come from** | Roots of denominator of $G(s)$ (or $G(s)H(s)$) | Roots of $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ |
| **Stability implication** | Plant stability | Overall system stability |
| **Effect of feedback** | Independent | Modified by loop gain $K$ and dynamics |
| **Mathematical operation** | $D(s) = 0$ | $D(s) + K \cdot N(s) = 0$ (typical) |

---

## 2.4 Stability Prediction from the Characteristic Equation

### Method 1: Direct Root Location (Factoring)
If the characteristic polynomial can be factored, the roots can be inspected directly:

$$s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6 = (s+1)(s+2)(s+3) = 0$$

All roots ($s = -1, -2, -3$) are negative real → **System is stable**.

### Method 2: Routh-Hurwitz Criterion
When direct factoring is difficult (high-order polynomial), construct the **Routh array**. A necessary (and for most practical cases sufficient) condition for stability is:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Routh-Hurwitz Necessity (preliminary check):**
> **All coefficients of the characteristic polynomial must be of the same sign and none can be zero.** If any coefficient is zero or signs change → at least one root lies in the RHP or on the imaginary axis → system is **unstable or marginally stable**.

> [!NOTE]
> The full Routh-Hurwitz array procedure and special cases (rows of zeros, $\epsilon$ technique) are covered in the next topic. For this module, focus on **constructing the characteristic equation** and **predicting stability from pole locations**.

### Method 3: Nyquist / Root Locus (Advanced)
- **Nyquist plot:** Encirclements of the critical point $(-1, 0)$ indicate instability.
- **Root locus:** Plots the trajectory of closed-loop poles as loop gain $K$ varies.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Open-loop transfer function | $G_{OL}(s) = N(s)/D(s)$ | Plant/process description |
| 2 | Open-loop characteristic equation | $D(s) = 0$ | Open-loop pole locations |
| 3 | Open-loop stability condition | All roots of $D(s)=0$ have Re$(s) < 0$ | Predict plant stability |
| 4 | Closed-loop transfer function (general) | $T(s) = G(s)/[1 + G(s)H(s)]$ | System response |
| 5 | **Closed-loop characteristic equation** | $\mathbf{1 + G(s)H(s) = 0}$ | **Most important for KTU** |
| 6 | Unity feedback form | $T(s) = G(s)/[1 + G(s)]$ | When $H(s) = 1$ |
| 7 | Expanded polynomial form | $D_p(s) + N_p(s) = 0$ | Factoring or Routh setup |
| 8 | BIBO stability | Bounded input → Bounded output | Time-domain test |
| 9 | Stable pole location | Re$(s) < 0$ (LHP) | s-plane condition |
| 10 | Coefficient sign rule (necessity) | All coefficients same sign, none zero | Quick preliminary check |

---

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of This Concept

- **Process industries (chemical, petrochemical):** Predicting whether a reactor temperature loop will oscillate or runaway.
- **Robotics:** Ensuring manipulator joint controllers don't diverge.
- **Aerospace:** Flight control systems where instability is catastrophic.
- **Biomedical:** Drug delivery infusion pumps — patient safety depends on closed-loop stability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Loop Transfer Function for Unity Feedback

**Starting point:** Block diagram with forward path $G(s)$, unity feedback, input $R(s)$, output $C(s)$, and error $E(s)$.

**Step 1:** The error signal is the difference between input and feedback:

$$E(s) = R(s) - C(s) \quad \text{(unity feedback } H(s) = 1\text{)}$$

**Step 2:** The forward path acts on the error to produce the output:

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute Step 1 into Step 2:

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot [R(s) - C(s)]$$

**Step 4:** Distribute the right-hand side:

$$C(s) = G(s) R(s) - G(s) C(s)$$

**Step 5:** Collect all $C(s)$ terms on the left-hand side:

$$C(s) + G(s) C(s) = G(s) R(s)$$

**Step 6:** Factor out $C(s)$:

$$C(s) \cdot [1 + G(s)] = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 7:** Solve for the ratio $C(s)/R(s)$:

$$\boxed{\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}}$$

This is the **closed-loop transfer function for unity negative feedback**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Closed-Loop Transfer Function for Non-Unity Feedback

**Starting point:** Forward path $G(s)$, feedback path $H(s)$, negative feedback.

**Step 1:** Error signal (general):

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) C(s)$$

**Step 2:** Forward path action:

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s) = G(s)[R(s) - H(s) C(s)]$$

**Step 3:** Distribute:

$$C(s) = G(s) R(s) - G(s) H(s) C(s)$$

**Step 4:** Collect $C(s)$ terms on the left:

$$C(s) + G(s) H(s) C(s) = G(s) R(s)$$

$$C(s)[1 + G(s)H(s)] = G(s) R(s)$$

**Step 5:** Final result:

$$\boxed{\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: The Characteristic Equation

Starting from the closed-loop transfer function with $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{N_p(s)}{D_p(s)}$:

**Step 1:** Substitute into the denominator:

$$1 + \frac{N_p(s)}{D_p(s)} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Combine over a common denominator:

$$\frac{D_p(s) + N_p(s)}{D_p(s)} = 0$$

**Step 3:** A fraction equals zero only when its numerator equals zero (denominator ≠ 0):

$$\boxed{D_p(s) + N_p(s) = 0}$$

This is the **expanded characteristic equation**.

**Step 4:** Its roots are the **closed-loop poles** and dictate system stability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Insight for KTU:**
> - Open-loop poles come from $D_p(s) = 0$.
> - Closed-loop poles come from $D_p(s) + N_p(s) = 0$.
> - The two are generally **different**, except in degenerate cases.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — End-to-End Stability Prediction

**Problem:** Given $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$ and unity feedback ($H(s)=1$), find:
1. The closed-loop transfer function
2. The characteristic equation
3. Stability condition on $K$
4. Stability for $K = 1$ and $K = 50$

### Solution

**Step 1: Closed-loop transfer function**

$$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) \cdot 1} = \frac{\frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}}{1 + \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}}$$

**Step 2: Simplify**

$$T(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4) + K}$$

**Step 3: Expand the denominator**

$$s(s+2)(s+4) = s(s^2 + 6s + 8) = s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s$$

So the **characteristic equation** is:

$$s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + K = 0$$

**Step 4: Apply coefficient sign rule (preliminary check)**

All coefficients ($1, 6, 8, K$) must be **positive** → need $K > 0$.

**Step 5: Stability test for $K = 1$**

Characteristic equation: $s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + 1 = 0$

Construct the **Routh array**:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | 1 | 8 | 0 |
| $s^2$ | 6 | 1 | 0 |
| $s^1$ | $\frac{6 \cdot 8 - 1 \cdot 1}{6} = \frac{47}{6} \approx 7.83$ | 0 | |
| $s^0$ | 1 | | |

All first-column entries are positive → **No sign changes → System is STABLE for $K = 1$.**

**Step 6: Stability test for $K = 50$**

Characteristic equation: $s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + 50 = 0$

Routh array:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | 1 | 8 |
| $s^2$ | 6 | 50 |
| $s^1$ | $\frac{6 \cdot 8 - 1 \cdot 50}{6} = \frac{-2}{6} = -\frac{1}{3}$ | 0 |
| $s^0$ | 50 | |

**Sign changes in first column:** $+\;1, +\;6, -\;1/3, +\;50$ → **Two sign changes** → **System is UNSTABLE for $K = 50$.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:**
> At $K = 1$, the loop gain is low and the natural damping of the plant dominates, keeping the response bounded. At $K = 50$, the controller acts too aggressively, amplifying noise and driving the system into self-sustained oscillation or divergence. This is the classic **gain-induced instability** phenomenon.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Special Cases to Remember

| Case | Characteristic Equation | Closed-Loop Pole Location | Stability |
|---|---|---|---|
| $G(s) = K/(s+a)$, $H=1$ | $s + a + K = 0$ | $s = -(a+K)$ | Stable if $a + K > 0$ |
| $G(s) = K/[s(s+a)]$, $H=1$ | $s^2 + as + K = 0$ | $s = \frac{-a \pm \sqrt{a^2 - 4K}}{2}$ | Stable if $a > 0, K > 0$ |
| $G(s) = \omega_n^2 / s^2$, $H=1$ | $s^2 + \omega_n^2 = 0$ | $s = \pm j\omega_n$ | Marginally stable (poles on jω-axis) |
| $G(s) = K/(s-2)$, $H=1$ | $s - 2 + K = 0$ | $s = 2 - K$ | Stable only if $K > 2$ |
| $G(s) = K/[s(s-1)]$, $H=1$ | $s^2 - s + K = 0$ | $s = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1-4K}}{2}$ | Unstable for all $K > 0$ |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Standard Unity Negative Feedback System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input R(s)] --> B[Summing Junction]
    B --> C[Error E(s)]
    C --> D["Forward Path G(s)"]
    D --> E[Output C(s)]
    E --> F[Feedback Path H(s)]
    F --> B
    H((Negative Sign)) -.-> B
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style E fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style F fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#6a1b9a
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The summing junction subtracts the feedback signal from the input.
- The forward path $G(s)$ processes the error to produce the output.
- The feedback path $H(s)$ senses the output and returns a fraction of it.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Disturbance Rejection Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[R(s) Input] --> B[Summing Junction 1]
    B --> C["Controller Gc(s)"]
    C --> D[Summing Junction 2]
    D --> E["Plant Gp(s)"]
    E --> F[C(s) Output]
    F --> G["Sensor H(s)"]
    G --> B
    P[Disturbance D(s)] --> D
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style D fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style E fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style F fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style G fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style P fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
```

> [!NOTE]
> The characteristic equation for this configuration is still $1 + G_c(s) G_p(s) H(s) = 0$, **independent of the disturbance location**. Disturbances affect the *output* but not the *stability* of the loop (in LTI systems).

---

## 4.3 s-Plane Pole Location Map — Stability Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["s-plane Diagram"]
    subgraph STABLE_REGION["LEFT HALF PLANE - STABLE"]
        direction LR
        P1[Pole 1: -2 + j0]
        P2[Pole 2: -1 + j1]
        P3[Pole 3: -1 - j1]
    end
    subgraph MARGINAL_REGION["IMAGINARY AXIS - MARGINALLY STABLE"]
        direction LR
        P4[Pole 4: 0 + j3]
        P5[Pole 5: 0 - j3]
    end
    subgraph UNSTABLE_REGION["RIGHT HALF PLANE - UNSTABLE"]
        direction LR
        P6[Pole 6: +1 + j0]
        P7[Pole 7: +0.5 + j2]
    end
    STABLE_REGION --> A
    MARGINAL_REGION --> A
    UNSTABLE_REGION --> A
    style STABLE_REGION fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style MARGINAL_REGION fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style UNSTABLE_REGION fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
```

**Interpretation of pole locations:**
- **LHP poles (negative real part):** Response decays to zero → stable.
- **jω-axis poles (zero real part):** Sustained oscillations → marginally stable.
- **RHP poles (positive real part):** Response grows unbounded → unstable.

---

## 4.4 Signal-Flow Graph of Closed-Loop System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1["R(s)"] ---|1| N2["E(s)"]
    N2 ---|G(s)| N3["C(s)"]
    N3 ---|H(s)| N4["B(s)"]
    N4 -->|subtract| N2
    style N1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style N2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style N3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style N4 fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#6a1b9a
```

**Mason's gain formula** (for parallel/feedback verification):

$$T(s) = \frac{P_1 \Delta_1}{\Delta} = \frac{G(s) \cdot 1}{1 - [-G(s)H(s)]} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

This confirms the closed-loop transfer function obtained via block diagram reduction.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
> **Define the characteristic equation of a closed-loop control system. How is it related to the closed-loop poles?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **characteristic equation** of a closed-loop control system is obtained by setting the denominator of the closed-loop transfer function equal to zero. Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$$

For a unity feedback system with $G(s) = N(s)/D(s)$, the characteristic equation becomes:

$$D(s) + N(s) = 0$$

The **roots of the characteristic equation are the closed-loop poles** of the system. The stability of the closed-loop system is determined entirely by the location of these roots in the s-plane:

- All roots in the **LHP** → **Stable system**
- Repeated roots on the **jω-axis** → **Unstable**
- Simple roots on the **jω-axis**, rest in LHP → **Marginally stable**
- At least one root in the **RHP** → **Unstable**

---

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
> **State the condition for stability of an open-loop system. Why is open-loop stability not sufficient to guarantee closed-loop stability?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Open-loop stability condition:** A linear open-loop system with transfer function $G(s) = N(s)/D(s)$ is **stable** if and only if **all poles (roots of $D(s) = 0$) lie in the left-half of the s-plane** (i.e., all poles have strictly negative real parts).

**Why open-loop stability is not sufficient:**
Even if a plant is open-loop stable, closing the feedback loop modifies the pole locations because the **closed-loop poles are roots of $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$**, which is generally different from $D(s) = 0$. Depending on the loop gain and the dynamics of $G(s)$ and $H(s)$:

1. A stable plant can become **unstable** when feedback is applied (e.g., excessive loop gain causes oscillation).
2. Conversely, an open-loop unstable plant can be made **stable** with a properly designed controller.

Hence, **closed-loop stability must be independently verified** by examining the roots of the characteristic equation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Pattern

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, Pattern Dec 2024]**
>
> **(a)** For a unity feedback control system with forward path transfer function:
>
> $$G(s) = \frac{K(s+4)}{s(s+2)(s+6)}$$
>
> Derive the closed-loop transfer function. Hence obtain the characteristic equation and apply the **Routh-Hurwitz criterion** to find the range of $K$ for stability. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A unity feedback system has $G(s) = \dfrac{20}{s(s+1)(s+5)}$. Determine the stability of the system using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion. Comment on the nature of instability, if any. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Step 1: Closed-loop transfer function**

For unity feedback, $H(s) = 1$:

$$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{K(s+4)}{s(s+2)(s+6)}}{1 + \dfrac{K(s+4)}{s(s+2)(s+6)}}$$

**Step 2: Simplify**

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $s(s+2)(s+6)$:

$$T(s) = \frac{K(s+4)}{s(s+2)(s+6) + K(s+4)}$$

**Step 3: Expand the denominator**

$$s(s+2)(s+6) = s(s^2 + 8s + 12) = s^3 + 8s^2 + 12s$$

$$K(s+4) = Ks + 4K$$

So the denominator is:

$$s^3 + 8s^2 + 12s + Ks + 4K = s^3 + 8s^2 + (12 + K)s + 4K$$

**Step 4: Characteristic equation**

$$\boxed{s^3 + 8s^2 + (12 + K)s + 4K = 0}$$

**Step 5: Apply Routh-Hurwitz**

Construct the Routh array:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | 1 | $12 + K$ |
| $s^2$ | 8 | $4K$ |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{8(12+K) - 1 \cdot 4K}{8}$ | 0 |
| $s^0$ | $4K$ | |

**Step 6: Compute the $s^1$ row entry**

$$b_1 = \frac{8(12 + K) - 4K}{8} = \frac{96 + 8K - 4K}{8} = \frac{96 + 4K}{8} = 12 + \frac{K}{2}$$

**Step 7: Apply stability conditions (all first-column entries > 0)**

- $1 > 0$ ✓
- $8 > 0$ ✓
- $12 + K/2 > 0$ → $K > -24$
- $4K > 0$ → $K > 0$

**Step 8: Combine conditions**

The most restrictive condition is $K > 0$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{K > 0 \quad \text{for stability}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $K$ is typically a controller gain (positive), the system is stable for all positive values of $K$. This is a *minimum-phase* open-loop system (no RHP poles) and the additional zero at $s = -4$ improves relative stability.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Step 1: Characteristic equation**

For unity feedback, $H(s) = 1$:

$$1 + G(s) = 0 \implies 1 + \frac{20}{s(s+1)(s+5)} = 0$$

$$s(s+1)(s+5) + 20 = 0$$

**Step 2: Expand**

$$s(s^2 + 6s + 5) + 20 = 0$$

$$s^3 + 6s^2 + 5s + 20 = 0$$

**Step 3: Routh array**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | 1 | 5 |
| $s^2$ | 6 | 20 |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{6 \cdot 5 - 1 \cdot 20}{6} = \dfrac{10}{6} = \dfrac{5}{3}$ | 0 |
| $s^0$ | 20 | |

**Step 4: First column**

$+1, +6, +5/3, +20$ → **All positive, no sign changes → System is STABLE.**

**Step 5: Frequency of oscillation (auxiliary equation from $s^2$ row)**

The $s^2$ row gives the auxiliary equation:

$$6s^2 + 20 = 0 \implies s^2 = -\frac{20}{6} = -\frac{10}{3}$$

$$s = \pm j\sqrt{\frac{10}{3}} \approx \pm j\,1.826 \text{ rad/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Wait — re-check: if no sign changes occur in the first column, the auxiliary equation does **not** indicate sustained oscillation. The auxiliary equation is meaningful only when a row becomes zero. Here, the system is **strictly stable with damped oscillations**, and the $s^1$ entry $5/3 > 0$ confirms that.

**Conclusion:** The system is **stable**, and all transient responses decay exponentially (no sustained oscillation).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Pattern (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, Pattern Dec 2024]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, derive the closed-loop transfer function of a **non-unity negative feedback** system. Hence define the characteristic equation and explain its significance in predicting closed-loop stability. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A process has the open-loop transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{2}{s-3}$ with unity feedback. Investigate the closed-loop stability. If the system is unstable, find the value of feedback gain $K_f$ (placed in the feedback path as $H(s) = K_f$) that makes the system stable. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Step 1: Block diagram and derivation**

Consider the non-unity negative feedback system with forward path $G(s)$, feedback path $H(s)$, input $R(s)$, output $C(s)$, feedback signal $B(s)$, and error $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$.

From the block diagram:

$$C(s) = G(s) E(s) = G(s)[R(s) - H(s) C(s)]$$

$$C(s) = G(s) R(s) - G(s) H(s) C(s)$$

$$C(s)[1 + G(s) H(s)] = G(s) R(s)$$

$$\boxed{\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}}$$

**Step 2: Define the characteristic equation**

Setting the denominator of $T(s)$ to zero:

$$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$$

Substituting $G(s)H(s) = N_p(s)/D_p(s)$:

$$D_p(s) + N_p(s) = 0$$

**Step 3: Significance in stability prediction**

The characteristic equation:
- **Determines the closed-loop poles** — the roots directly give the natural modes of the system.
- **Encodes all dynamic information** about the closed-loop system (response speed, damping, oscillation).
- **Allows the use of algebraic criteria** (Routh-Hurwitz) to check stability without explicitly computing the roots.
- **Is independent of the input** — stability is an *intrinsic* system property.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:**
> A 7-mark answer must include the **block diagram**, the **full derivation** (not just stating the result), the **characteristic equation definition**, and at least **two to three significance points** with engineering context.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Step 1: Given data**

- $G(s) = \dfrac{2}{s - 3}$ (open-loop unstable plant; pole at $s = +3$ in RHP)
- $H(s) = 1$ (unity feedback, initial case)
- $H(s) = K_f$ (non-unity feedback for stabilization)

**Case 1: Unity feedback ($H(s) = 1$)**

**Step 2: Closed-loop transfer function**

$$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{2}{s-3}}{1 + \dfrac{2}{s-3}} = \frac{2}{s - 3 + 2} = \frac{2}{s - 1}$$

**Step 3: Characteristic equation**

$$1 + G(s) = 0 \implies s - 1 = 0 \implies s = +1$$

**Step 4: Stability check**

Pole at $s = +1$ (RHP) → **System is UNSTABLE** with unity feedback.

**Case 2: Non-unity feedback ($H(s) = K_f$)**

**Step 5: Closed-loop transfer function**

$$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{2}{s-3}}{1 + \dfrac{2 K_f}{s-3}} = \frac{2}{s - 3 + 2K_f}$$

**Step 6: Characteristic equation**

$$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0 \implies s - 3 + 2K_f = 0 \implies s = 3 - 2K_f$$

**Step 7: Stability condition**

For closed-loop stability, the closed-loop pole must be in the LHP:

$$\text{Re}(s) < 0 \implies 3 - 2K_f < 0 \implies K_f > \frac{3}{2}$$

$$\boxed{K_f > 1.5 \quad \text{for stability}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Even an open-loop unstable plant (RHP pole at $s = +3$) can be stabilized by choosing an appropriate feedback gain. The feedback action effectively *shifts* the pole from $s = +3$ (RHP) to $s = 3 - 2K_f$ (LHP when $K_f > 1.5$). This is a powerful illustration of how feedback control can rescue an otherwise unusable process.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic (Based on KTU Valuation Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Confusing the characteristic equation with the denominator of $G(s)$ alone.** The characteristic equation is $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$, **not** $G(s) = 0$ or $D(s) = 0$.
> 2. **Missing the feedback sign.** KTU questions always assume **negative feedback** unless explicitly stated. Using $1 - G(s)H(s)$ in the denominator leads to a wrong characteristic equation.
> 3. **Forgetting to clear the fraction.** When writing $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ with $G(s)H(s) = N/D$, students sometimes forget to multiply by $D(s)$ to obtain $D + N = 0$.
> 4. **Incomplete derivation.** Part (a) of a 7-mark question demands a full algebraic derivation with at least 4–5 steps. Just writing the final answer fetches only 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Not commenting on the nature of stability.** When a system is stable, students should also state whether the response is *overdamped*, *underdamped*, or *critically damped* (based on the $s^1$ row sign of the Routh array).
> 6. **Not specifying the value of $K$ range for stability.** A complete answer must state the inequality (e.g., $0 < K < 24$) clearly, not just "K must be positive."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Stability Prediction**
>
> - **Stability = all closed-loop poles in LHP** (BIBO criterion).
> - **Open-loop stability:** roots of $D(s) = 0$ all in LHP.
> - **Closed-loop stability:** roots of $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ all in LHP.
> - **Characteristic equation:** $\boxed{1 + G(s)H(s) = 0}$ — the **most important equation** in this module.
> - **Expanded form:** $D_p(s) + N_p(s) = 0$ after clearing fractions.
> - **Closed-loop poles ≠ Open-loop poles** in general.
> - **Preliminary sign check:** all coefficients of the characteristic polynomial must be **same sign, none zero**.
> - **Routh array** is the systematic method to test stability without finding roots.
> - **Number of sign changes** in the first column of the Routh array = number of roots in the RHP.
> - **Marginal stability** occurs when a row of the Routh array becomes zero (auxiliary equation method needed).
> - **Feedback can stabilize an unstable plant** (e.g., $G(s) = 2/(s-3)$ stabilized with $K_f > 1.5$).
> - **Feedback can destabilize a stable plant** if loop gain is too high.
> - For KTU exams, **always show full derivation**, **state the range of $K$**, and **comment on the nature of response**.

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Routh-Hurwitz Stability Criterion

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
The Routh-Hurwitz criterion is an algebraic necessary and sufficient condition for assessing the asymptotic stability of a linear time-invariant (LTI) system by examining the coefficients of the characteristic polynomial **P(s) = a₀sⁿ + a₁sⁿ⁻¹ + a₂sⁿ⁻² + ... + aₙ₋₁s + aₙ = 0** without explicitly computing its roots. The criterion establishes that a system is stable if and only if all elements of the first column of the Routh array have the same sign (typically, all positive for positive leading coefficient).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this needed?** Solving a 4th or 5th order polynomial analytically is mathematically tedious. Routh-Hurwitz provides a quick, tabular method to determine:
> 1. Whether **all roots lie in the left-half s-plane** (stable).
> 2. The **number of roots in the right-half s-plane** (count of sign changes in first column).
> 3. The **critical gain (Kc)** at which the system becomes marginally stable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Mountain Climber Test:**
> Imagine a closed-loop control system as a ball placed in a bowl (stable) versus a ball balanced on top of a hill (unstable). Instead of pushing the ball and watching what happens (solving the differential equation = time-consuming), the Routh-Hurwitz criterion is like mathematically checking the **curvature (sign of coefficients)** and **second-derivative conditions (Routh array)** of the bowl/hill at the equilibrium point. If the geometry is "bowl-shaped" (all first-column entries positive), the ball will roll back to the center — **stable**. If any sign reverses, the geometry has a "hill-shape" component — **unstable**.

### 1.2 Crossover Frequencies, Gain Margin, and Phase Margin

**Formal Definitions:**

- **Gain Crossover Frequency (ω_gc):** The frequency on the Bode magnitude plot at which the open-loop magnitude **|G(jω)H(jω)| = 1** (equivalently, **0 dB**). Mathematically, it is the solution of **20 log₁₀ |G(jω_gc)H(jω_gc)| = 0**.

- **Phase Crossover Frequency (ω_pc):** The frequency at which the open-loop phase angle **∠G(jω)H(jω) = -180°** (or -π rad). It is the solution of the phase equation.

- **Gain Margin (GM):** A measure of how much the open-loop gain can be increased before the closed-loop system reaches the verge of instability. It is defined as:
  > **GM = 1 / |G(jω_pc)H(jω_pc)|**  or in decibels: **GM_dB = -20 log₁₀ |G(jω_pc)H(jω_pc)|**

- **Phase Margin (PM):** A measure of additional phase lag (at the gain crossover) the system can tolerate before becoming unstable. Defined as:
  > **PM = 180° + ∠G(jω_gc)H(jω_gc)**

### 1.3 Bode Stability Criterion

**Formal Definition:**
For a **minimum-phase** open-loop system (no poles or zeros in the right-half plane), the Nyquist contour encircles the critical point (-1, 0) the same number of times as the Bode plot's gain and phase plots cross certain thresholds. The **Bode Stability Criterion** states:

> A closed-loop system is **stable** if and only if, at the **gain crossover frequency (ω_gc)**, the phase angle is **greater than -180°** (i.e., PM > 0). Equivalently, at the **phase crossover frequency (ω_pc)**, the magnitude is **less than 0 dB** (i.e., GM > 1 or GM_dB > 0).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Highway Overtaking Test:**
> Picture driving on a curved highway. The **gain margin** is how much extra *speed* (gain) you can add before the car skids off the curve at the critical sharp turn (phase crossover). The **phase margin** is how much *extra steering lag* (phase delay) you can introduce before losing control at your normal cruising speed. For a safe journey (stability), you need **positive** margin in both — meaning you must cross the 0 dB line (normal speed) *before* hitting the -180° phase line (sharp turn).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Routh-Hurwitz Criterion — Theoretical Framework

The criterion is built on two layers: a **necessary condition** and a **sufficient condition**.

#### Layer 1: Necessary Condition (Hurwitz Test)
For a polynomial with real coefficients, **all coefficients must be non-zero and have the same sign** (positive, assuming a₀ > 0). If even one coefficient is zero or negative, the system has right-half-plane (RHP) poles or is on the imaginary axis (marginally stable).

**Example failure:** Polynomial s³ + s² - 2s + 5 = 0 has a negative coefficient, hence immediately declared unstable.

#### Layer 2: Sufficient Condition (Routh Array Construction)

Given **P(s) = a₀sⁿ + a₁sⁿ⁻¹ + a₂sⁿ⁻² + ... + aₙ = 0** with a₀ > 0, the Routh array is constructed as:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 | ... |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| sⁿ | a₀ | a₂ | a₄ | ... |
| sⁿ⁻¹ | a₁ | a₃ | a₅ | ... |
| sⁿ⁻² | b₁ | b₂ | b₃ | ... |
| sⁿ⁻³ | c₁ | c₂ | c₃ | ... |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| s⁰ | ... | ... | ... | ... |

**Recursive Formulas:**

$$
b_1 = \frac{a_1 a_2 - a_0 a_3}{a_1}, \quad b_2 = \frac{a_1 a_4 - a_0 a_5}{a_1}
$$

$$
c_1 = \frac{b_1 a_3 - a_1 b_2}{b_1}, \quad c_2 = \frac{b_1 a_5 - a_1 b_3}{b_1}
$$

The pattern generalizes: **Each element = [(upper row first col × lower row col k+1) − (lower row first col × upper row col k+1)] ÷ (lower row first col)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Decision Rule:** The number of roots with positive real parts equals the **number of sign changes** in the first column of the Routh array. If all first-column entries have the **same sign** (and are non-zero), the system is **completely stable**.

#### Special Cases in Routh Array

**Case 1: First column element is zero (with non-zero row):**
Substitute a small positive number **ε → 0⁺** and continue the construction. The presence of ε indicates proximity to the imaginary axis.

**Case 2: Entire row becomes zero:**
This indicates **symmetrically placed roots** about the origin. Form an **auxiliary polynomial** A(s) from the row above, take its derivative dA/ds, and use the coefficients of dA/ds to replace the zero row.

### 2.2 Frequency Domain Stability Margins — Theoretical Framework

**Construction of Bode Plot:**
1. Substitute **s = jω** into G(s)H(s).
2. Compute **magnitude** M(ω) = \vert G(jω)H(jω) \vert and **phase** φ(ω) = ∠G(jω)H(jω).
3. Use **asymptotic approximations** by replacing each term:
   - **(jω + a)** → corner frequency at ω = a, slope changes by ±20 dB/dec.
   - **(1 + jωτ)** → corner at ω = 1/τ.
   - **1/(jω)** → slope of -20 dB/dec from origin.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Characteristic Equation | a₀sⁿ + a₁sⁿ⁻¹ + ... + aₙ = 0 | Roots determine stability |
| 2 | Necessary Condition | All aᵢ > 0 (for a₀ > 0) | Hurwitz necessary test |
| 3 | Routh Array Element | b₁ = (a₁a₂ − a₀a₃)/a₁ | Recursive determinant pattern |
| 4 | Sign Changes in 1st Column | = Number of RHP roots | Stability decision rule |
| 5 | Gain Crossover Frequency | \vert G(jω_gc)H(jω_gc) \vert = 1 | Solution of 0 dB crossing |
| 6 | Phase Crossover Frequency | ∠G(jω_pc)H(jω_pc) = -180° | Solution of phase equation |
| 7 | Gain Margin (linear) | GM = 1 / \vert G(jω_pc)H(jω_pc) \vert | Dimensionless ratio |
| 8 | Gain Margin (dB) | GM_dB = -20 log₁₀ \vert G(jω_pc)H(jω_pc) \vert | Positive ⇒ Stable |
| 9 | Phase Margin | PM = 180° + ∠G(jω_gc)H(jω_gc) | Positive ⇒ Stable |
| 10 | Bode Stability Criterion (Gain) | At ω_gc : φ > -180° | Equivalent to PM > 0 |
| 11 | Bode Stability Criterion (Phase) | At ω_pc : \vert G \vert < 1 (0 dB) | Equivalent to GM > 1 |
| 12 | Sustained Oscillation Frequency | From auxiliary s² row: s² = -ω² | Imaginary axis crossing |
| 13 | Critical Gain Kc | Value of K when s¹ row = 0 | Boundary of stability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Routh-Hurwitz is widely used in **chemical process control** (stability of exothermic reactors), **power systems** (swing equation stability), and **aerospace** (flight controller design) where the system order is high (≥ 4) and root-finding is impractical. Gain and phase margins are universally used in **PID tuning** to guarantee robustness against plant uncertainty (typical design targets: GM ≥ 6 dB, PM ≥ 30°–60°).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Routh-Hurwitz — Finding Range of K for Stability

**Problem Statement:**
A unity feedback control system has an open-loop transfer function:

$$
G(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}
$$

Determine:
1. The range of K for which the closed-loop system is **stable**.
2. The value of K that produces **sustained oscillations**, and the corresponding **frequency of oscillation**.

#### Step 1: Form the Characteristic Equation
For unity negative feedback: 1 + G(s) = 0

$$
1 + \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)} = 0
$$

$$
s(s+2)(s+4) + K = 0
$$

Expand the product:

$$
s \cdot (s^2 + 6s + 8) + K = 0
$$

$$
s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + K = 0
$$

#### Step 2: Apply the Necessary Condition
Coefficients: **a₀ = 1, a₁ = 6, a₂ = 8, a₃ = K**. All must be positive ⇒ **K > 0**.

#### Step 3: Construct the Routh Array

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|---|---|---|
| s³ | 1 | 8 |
| s² | 6 | K |
| s¹ | b₁ | 0 |
| s⁰ | c₁ | 0 |

Compute **b₁** using the Routh formula:

$$
b_1 = \frac{(6)(8) - (1)(K)}{6} = \frac{48 - K}{6}
$$

Compute **c₁** (using s² row and s¹ row):

$$
c_1 = \frac{b_1 \cdot K - 6 \cdot 0}{b_1} = K
$$

#### Step 4: Apply the Stability Decision Rule
For all first-column entries to be positive:

$$
1 > 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
6 > 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
b_1 = \frac{48 - K}{6} > 0 \Rightarrow K < 48
$$

$$
c_1 = K > 0 \quad (\text{from Step 2})
$$

**Combined range: 0 < K < 48**

#### Step 5: Find Critical Gain and Oscillation Frequency
At the boundary, the s¹ row becomes zero ⇒ K = Kc = **48**.

Form the auxiliary polynomial from the s² row:

$$
A(s) = 6s^2 + K = 0
$$

Substitute K = 48:

$$
6s^2 + 48 = 0 \Rightarrow s^2 = -8 \Rightarrow s = \pm j\sqrt{8}
$$

Hence the frequency of sustained oscillation is:

$$
\boxed{\omega_n = \sqrt{8} = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.828 \text{ rad/s}}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Bode Stability Criterion — Gain and Phase Margins

**Problem Statement:**
For the open-loop transfer function:

$$
G(s)H(s) = \frac{10}{s(s+1)(s+5)}
$$

Calculate:
1. Gain crossover frequency ω_gc.
2. Phase crossover frequency ω_pc.
3. Gain Margin (GM) and Phase Margin (PM).
4. Comment on closed-loop stability.

#### Step 1: Substitute s = jω

$$
G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = \frac{10}{j\omega(1+j\omega)(5+j\omega)}
$$

#### Step 2: Magnitude Expression

$$
M(\omega) = \vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{10}{\omega \cdot \sqrt{1+\omega^2} \cdot \sqrt{25+\omega^2}}
$$

In decibels:

$$
M_{dB}(\omega) = 20\log_{10}(10) - 20\log_{10}\omega - 10\log_{10}(1+\omega^2) - 10\log_{10}(25+\omega^2)
$$

#### Step 3: Gain Crossover Frequency (ω_gc)
At ω_gc, M(ω_gc) = 1 ⇒ M_dB = 0 dB.

Approximate using asymptotic slopes for **low-to-mid frequency** (ω between 1 and 5):

For **ω in (1, 5)**: slope = -20 (from integrator) - 20 (from 1/(1+jω)) = -40 dB/dec.

At ω = 1 rad/s, magnitude is roughly:

$$
M_{dB}(1) = 20 - 0 - 0 - 10\log_{10}(26) \approx 20 - 14.15 = 5.85 \text{ dB}
$$

For -40 dB/dec slope from 5.85 dB at ω = 1:

$$
0 = 5.85 - 40 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\omega_{gc}}{1}\right) \Rightarrow \log_{10}\omega_{gc} = 0.146
$$

$$
\omega_{gc} \approx 1.40 \text{ rad/s}
$$

#### Step 4: Phase Expression

$$
\phi(\omega) = -90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega) - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega}{5}\right)
$$

#### Step 5: Phase Crossover Frequency (ω_pc)
Set φ(ω_pc) = -180°:

$$
-90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega_{pc}) - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega_{pc}}{5}\right) = -180°
$$

$$
\tan^{-1}(\omega_{pc}) + \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega_{pc}}{5}\right) = 90°
$$

Using the identity, this requires the denominator condition:

$$
1 - \frac{\omega_{pc} \cdot \omega_{pc}}{5} = 0 \Rightarrow \omega_{pc}^2 = 5
$$

$$
\boxed{\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236 \text{ rad/s}}
$$

#### Step 6: Compute Gain Margin
At ω_pc = √5:

$$
M(\omega_{pc}) = \frac{10}{\sqrt{5} \cdot \sqrt{1+5} \cdot \sqrt{25+5}} = \frac{10}{\sqrt{5} \cdot \sqrt{6} \cdot \sqrt{30}}
$$

$$
M(\omega_{pc}) = \frac{10}{\sqrt{5 \times 6 \times 30}} = \frac{10}{\sqrt{900}} = \frac{10}{30} = \frac{1}{3}
$$

$$
GM = \frac{1}{M(\omega_{pc})} = 3
$$

$$
\boxed{GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(3) \approx 9.54 \text{ dB (positive)}}
$$

#### Step 7: Compute Phase Margin
At ω_gc ≈ 1.40 rad/s:

$$
\phi(\omega_{gc}) = -90° - \tan^{-1}(1.40) - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1.40}{5}\right)
$$

$$
\phi(\omega_{gc}) = -90° - 54.46° - 15.64° = -160.10°
$$

$$
\boxed{PM = 180° + (-160.10°) = 19.90° \text{ (positive)}}
$$

#### Step 8: Stability Verdict
Since **GM_dB = +9.54 dB > 0** and **PM = +19.9° > 0**, both Bode stability criteria are satisfied ⇒ **Closed-loop system is STABLE**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Note:** The system is stable but has a low phase margin (~20°), indicating **oscillatory transient response** with high overshoot. In industrial practice, the gain K would be reduced to improve PM to at least 45° for satisfactory response.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for Stability

| Step | Check | Outcome | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | All characteristic equation coefficients non-zero and same sign? | No | System is unstable or marginally stable — STOP |
| 2 | Routh array constructed completely without zero rows? | Yes | Continue |
| 3 | First column all same sign? | Yes | System is STABLE |
| 4 | First column has sign changes? | Count them | That many poles are in RHP |
| 5 | s¹ row first element = 0 (for 2nd-order auxiliary)? | Yes | Marginal stability: find Kc and ωn |
| 6 | Entire row of zeros? | Yes | Use auxiliary polynomial A(s) and dA/ds |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Routh-Hurwitz Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[START: Characteristic Equation] --> A2{Coefficients all non-zero and same sign?}
    A2 -->|No| A3[UNSTABLE or MARGINAL: STOP]
    A2 -->|Yes| A4[Construct Routh Array Row by Row]
    A4 --> A5{Any entire row of zeros?}
    A5 -->|Yes| A6[Form Auxiliary Polynomial A s]
    A6 --> A7[Replace zero row with coefficients of dA/ds]
    A7 --> A8[Continue construction]
    A5 -->|No| A8
    A8 --> A9{Any zero in first column?}
    A9 -->|Yes| A10[Substitute epsilon and continue]
    A10 --> A11[Count sign changes in first column]
    A9 -->|No| A11
    A11 --> A12{Zero sign changes?}
    A12 -->|Yes| A13[SYSTEM IS STABLE: All LHP roots]
    A12 -->|No| A14[Count of sign changes equals RHP poles]
    A14 --> A15[SYSTEM IS UNSTABLE]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Bode Stability Criterion Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Open Loop Transfer Function GsHs] --> B2[Substitute s is j omega]
    B2 --> B3[Compute Magnitude M omega in dB]
    B2 --> B4[Compute Phase phi omega in degrees]
    B3 --> B5[Frequency Response Plot]
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> B6[Find omega gc: where M equals 0 dB]
    B5 --> B7[Find omega pc: where phi equals minus 180]
    B6 --> B8[Compute PM is 180 plus phi at omega gc]
    B7 --> B9[Compute GM is 1 over M at omega pc]
    B8 --> B10{PM is positive?}
    B9 --> B11{GM is positive?}
    B10 -->|Yes| B12[STABLE]
    B10 -->|No| B13[UNSTABLE]
    B11 -->|Yes| B12
    B11 -->|No| B13
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Stability Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C1[Input: System Transfer Function] --> C2{Analysis Domain?}
    C2 -->|Time / s-domain| C3[Routh Hurwitz Method]
    C2 -->|Frequency / j-omega| C4[Bode Plot Method]
    C3 --> C5[Routh Array Construction]
    C5 --> C6[First Column Sign Analysis]
    C6 --> C7[Stability Verdict and RHP pole count]
    C4 --> C8[Asymptotic Magnitude Plot]
    C4 --> C9[Phase Plot]
    C8 --> C10[Identify omega gc and omega pc]
    C9 --> C10
    C10 --> C11[Compute GM and PM]
    C11 --> C12[Stability Verdict and Robustness Margin]
    C7 --> C13[Final Output: Stability Status plus Kc and omega n]
    C12 --> C13
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Stability Margins — Geometric Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Stability Margins on Bode Plot] --> D2[GAIN MARGIN GM]
    D1 --> D3[PHASE MARGIN PM]
    D2 --> D4[Vertical distance from magnitude curve to 0 dB axis at omega pc]
    D3 --> D5[Horizontal distance from phase curve to minus 180 line at omega gc]
    D4 --> D6[Positive GM means 0 dB crossing occurs before phase crosses minus 180]
    D5 --> D7[Positive PM means phase is above minus 180 when magnitude equals 0 dB]
    D6 --> D8[STABLE Closed Loop]
    D7 --> D8
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State the Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion. Why is it preferred over direct root computation for high-order systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion is an algebraic procedure that determines the asymptotic stability of an LTI system by examining the coefficients of the characteristic equation **P(s) = a₀sⁿ + a₁sⁿ⁻¹ + ... + aₙ = 0** and constructing a tabular array (Routh array) from them. A system is stable if and only if **all elements of the first column of the Routh array have the same sign** (typically positive), and the number of sign changes in the first column equals the number of poles in the right-half s-plane.

It is preferred over direct root computation for high-order systems because:
- **(1 mark)** Solving a 4th or higher order polynomial analytically is mathematically tedious and often requires numerical methods.
- **(1 mark)** Routh-Hurwitz provides a **systematic, non-computational, and exact** answer regarding stability without needing to find individual roots.
- **(1 mark)** It also yields the **critical gain Kc** and oscillation frequency **ωn** at the stability boundary — useful for controller design.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define gain margin and phase margin. State the Bode stability criterion for minimum-phase systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Gain Margin (GM):** It is the factor by which the open-loop gain can be increased at the phase crossover frequency before the closed-loop system becomes unstable. Mathematically, **GM = 1 / |G(jω_pc)H(jω_pc)|** or in dB, **GM_dB = -20 log₁₀|G(jω_pc)H(jω_pc)|**. **(1 mark)**
- **Phase Margin (PM):** It is the additional phase lag (in degrees) that can be tolerated at the gain crossover frequency before instability. **PM = 180° + ∠G(jω_gc)H(jω_gc)**. **(1 mark)**
- **Bode Stability Criterion:** For a minimum-phase system, the closed-loop system is stable if and only if **both the gain margin and phase margin are positive**, i.e., the gain crossover frequency occurs before the phase crossover frequency. Equivalently, **PM > 0° and GM > 1 (GM_dB > 0)**. **(1 mark)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** In Routh array construction, students often forget to **shift columns** correctly when the highest-order row has fewer elements. The pattern is always: `(upper_row_first_col × lower_row_next_col − lower_row_first_col × upper_row_next_col) / lower_row_first_col`. Marks lost: **2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Students treat **ω_gc** and **ω_pc** interchangeably. **ω_gc** is the 0 dB crossing of the **magnitude plot**; **ω_pc** is the -180° crossing of the **phase plot**. Marks lost: **1–2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In Bode plot analysis, the phase margin formula is **180° + φ(ω_gc)**, not **−180° + φ(ω_gc)**. Marks lost: **1 mark**.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** For special Routh cases (zero row, first column zero), students often declare the system stable or unstable without completing the **auxiliary polynomial** step. This is a **mandatory 2–3 mark** deduction.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice Option 1

**Q.A. (a) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**
The characteristic equation of a closed-loop control system is given by:
**s⁴ + 2s³ + 3s² + 4s + K = 0**
Determine:
(i) The range of K for system stability using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion.
(ii) The value of K for marginal stability and the corresponding frequency of oscillation.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Apply Hurwitz Necessary Condition (1 mark)**
All coefficients must be positive. Coefficients: 1, 2, 3, 4, K.
⇒ **K > 0**.

**Step 2: Construct Routh Array (3 marks)**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| s⁴ | 1 | 3 | K |
| s³ | 2 | 4 | 0 |
| s² | b₁ | b₂ | 0 |
| s¹ | c₁ | 0 | 0 |
| s⁰ | d₁ | 0 | 0 |

Compute b₁:
$$
b_1 = \frac{(2)(3) - (1)(4)}{2} = \frac{6 - 4}{2} = 1
$$

Compute b₂:
$$
b_2 = \frac{(2)(K) - (1)(0)}{2} = K
$$

Compute c₁:
$$
c_1 = \frac{(1)(4) - (2)(K)}{1} = 4 - 2K
$$

Compute d₁:
$$
d_1 = \frac{(4-2K)(K) - (1)(0)}{4-2K} = K
$$

**Step 3: Apply Stability Condition (1 mark)**
First column entries: 1, 2, 1, (4-2K), K
All must be positive:
- 4 − 2K > 0 ⇒ **K < 2**
- K > 0 (from Step 1)

**Range of K for stability: 0 < K < 2** **(1 mark)**

**Step 4: Marginal Stability Analysis (1 mark)**
At K = 2, the s¹ row becomes zero. Form the auxiliary polynomial from the s² row:
$$
A(s) = s^2 + K = 0 \Rightarrow s^2 = -2 \Rightarrow s = \pm j\sqrt{2}
$$

**Critical gain Kc = 2; Oscillation frequency ωn = √2 ≈ 1.414 rad/s** **(1 mark)**

---

**Q.A. (b) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**
For a unity feedback system with open-loop transfer function:
**G(s) = 20 / [s(s+2)(s+5)]**
Determine:
(i) Gain crossover frequency (ω_gc) and phase crossover frequency (ω_pc).
(ii) Gain margin and phase margin in dB/degrees.
(iii) Comment on closed-loop stability using the Bode stability criterion.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Frequency Response Function (1 mark)**
$$
G(j\omega) = \frac{20}{j\omega(2+j\omega)(5+j\omega)}
$$

**Step 2: Gain Crossover Frequency ω_gc (1 mark)**
Condition: |G(jω_gc)| = 1
$$
\frac{20}{\omega_{gc}\sqrt{4+\omega_{gc}^2}\sqrt{25+\omega_{gc}^2}} = 1
$$

For approximate solution in the range **2 < ω_gc < 5**, use asymptotes with slope -40 dB/dec past ω = 2:
At ω = 2, |G| ≈ 20/(2 × 2 × 5) = 1. Hence **ω_gc ≈ 2 rad/s** (more precisely, solving the equation gives ω_gc ≈ 1.85 rad/s).

**Step 3: Phase Crossover Frequency ω_pc (1 mark)**
Condition: ∠G(jω_pc) = -180°
$$
-90° - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega_{pc}}{2}\right) - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega_{pc}}{5}\right) = -180°
$$

Using the identity for arctan sum, this requires:
$$
1 - \frac{\omega_{pc}^2}{10} = 0 \Rightarrow \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 4: Gain Margin (1 mark)**
At ω_pc = √10:
$$
|G(j\omega_{pc})| = \frac{20}{\sqrt{10}\sqrt{4+10}\sqrt{25+10}} = \frac{20}{\sqrt{10}\sqrt{14}\sqrt{35}} = \frac{20}{\sqrt{4900}} = \frac{20}{70} = \frac{2}{7}
$$

$$
GM = \frac{1}{2/7} = 3.5 \Rightarrow \boxed{GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(3.5) \approx 10.88 \text{ dB}}
$$

**Step 5: Phase Margin (1 mark)**
At ω_gc ≈ 2 rad/s:
$$
\phi(\omega_{gc}) = -90° - \tan^{-1}(1) - \tan^{-1}(0.4) = -90° - 45° - 21.8° = -156.8°
$$

$$
\boxed{PM = 180° - 156.8° = 23.2°}
$$

**Step 6: Stability Verdict (1 mark)**
Since **GM_dB ≈ +10.88 dB > 0** and **PM ≈ +23.2° > 0**, both Bode stability conditions are satisfied ⇒ **Closed-loop system is STABLE**. However, PM is low (~23°), indicating an oscillatory transient response; reduction of gain K is recommended for better damping.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice Option 2

**Q.B. (a) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]**
**(a)** Explain the Bode stability criterion with the help of a neat sketch. Discuss the significance of gain margin and phase margin in the design of control systems.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Bode Stability Criterion Definition (2 marks)**
For a minimum-phase open-loop system whose transfer function has **no poles or zeros in the right-half s-plane**, the closed-loop system is stable if and only if the **gain crossover occurs at a lower frequency than the phase crossover**. Equivalently:
- The **phase margin (PM)** at the gain crossover frequency is **positive** (phase > -180° at ω_gc).
- The **gain margin (GM)** at the phase crossover frequency is **positive** (magnitude < 0 dB at ω_pc).

**Step 2: Sketch Description (2 marks)**
[Student should draw a Bode plot with:]
- Magnitude plot crossing 0 dB at ω_gc.
- Phase plot crossing -180° at ω_pc, with ω_gc < ω_pc for stability.
- GM marked as **vertical distance from magnitude curve to 0 dB line at ω_pc**.
- PM marked as **horizontal distance from phase curve to -180° line at ω_gc** (or vertical distance in phase).

**Step 3: Significance of GM and PM (3 marks)**
- **Gain Margin (GM):** Indicates the factor by which the system gain can be increased before reaching instability. A larger GM implies greater **robustness against gain variations** due to plant parameter drift. Typical design: **GM ≥ 6 dB**.
- **Phase Margin (PM):** Indicates the system's **damping characteristic** and overshoot. A larger PM implies **lower overshoot, less oscillation, and faster settling**. Typical design: **PM = 30°–60°** for well-damped response.
- Together, GM and PM quantify the **relative stability** of a closed-loop system. They are essential for tuning **PID controllers** in industrial process control to balance **speed of response** vs **stability margin**.

---

**Q.B. (b) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]**
The open-loop transfer function of a unity feedback system is:
**G(s) = K / [s(s+1)(s+2)]**
Use the Routh-Hurwitz criterion to determine:
(i) The range of K for which the system is stable.
(ii) The value of K and frequency at which the system is marginally stable.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Form the Characteristic Equation (1 mark)**
$$
1 + G(s) = 0 \Rightarrow s(s+1)(s+2) + K = 0
$$

Expand:
$$
s(s^2 + 3s + 2) + K = s^3 + 3s^2 + 2s + K = 0
$$

**Step 2: Construct Routh Array (2 marks)**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|---|---|---|
| s³ | 1 | 2 |
| s² | 3 | K |
| s¹ | (6-K)/3 | 0 |
| s⁰ | K | 0 |

**Step 3: Apply Stability Condition (2 marks)**
- From s¹ row: (6 − K)/3 > 0 ⇒ **K < 6**
- From s⁰ row: K > 0
- From s², s³ rows: always positive

**Range of K: 0 < K < 6**

**Step 4: Marginal Stability (2 marks)**
At K = Kc = 6, s¹ row becomes zero. Form auxiliary polynomial from s² row:
$$
A(s) = 3s^2 + K = 0 \Rightarrow 3s^2 + 6 = 0 \Rightarrow s^2 = -2
$$

$$
s = \pm j\sqrt{2}
$$

**Critical gain Kc = 6; Frequency of sustained oscillation ωn = √2 ≈ 1.414 rad/s**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4 Stability Analysis**

- **Routh-Hurwitz Criterion** determines asymptotic stability of LTI systems **without solving** the characteristic polynomial. **(High frequency in KTU exams)**
- **Necessary condition** for stability: All coefficients of the characteristic equation must be **non-zero and have the same sign** (positive for positive a₀).
- **Sufficient condition**: All entries in the **first column of the Routh array** must have the **same sign** (positive).
- **Number of RHP poles = Number of sign changes** in the first column of the Routh array.
- **Special Case 1**: If the **first element of a row is zero** (but row is not all zero), substitute a small **ε → 0⁺** and continue.
- **Special Case 2**: If an **entire row is zero**, the system has symmetrically placed roots. Form the **auxiliary polynomial A(s)** from the row above, take **dA/ds**, and replace the zero row with its coefficients.
- **Critical Gain Kc** is the value of K that makes the s¹ row (or first element of any odd-power row) equal to **zero**.
- **Frequency of sustained oscillation** is found from the auxiliary polynomial A(s) = 0 by setting s = jω and solving for **ωn**.
- **Gain Crossover Frequency (ω_gc)**: Frequency at which **|G(jω)H(jω)| = 1 (0 dB)**. Found on the **magnitude plot**.
- **Phase Crossover Frequency (ω_pc)**: Frequency at which **∠G(jω)H(jω) = -180°**. Found on the **phase plot**.
- **Gain Margin (GM)** = 1 / |G(jω_pc)H(jω_pc)|, in dB: **GM_dB = -20 log|G(jω_pc)H(jω_pc)|**. **Positive ⇒ Stable.**
- **Phase Margin (PM)** = 180° + ∠G(jω_gc)H(jω_gc). **Positive ⇒ Stable.**
- **Bode Stability Criterion** (for minimum-phase systems): A closed-loop system is stable iff the gain crossover occurs **before** the phase crossover. Mathematically: **PM > 0° AND GM > 1 (GM_dB > 0)**.
- **Industrial design targets**: GM ≥ 6 dB, PM = 30°–60° for well-damped transient response.
- **Bode plot corner frequencies** are at the pole/zero break points (e.g., for 1/(1+sτ), corner is at ω = 1/τ). Slope changes by **±20 dB/decade** at each corner.
- **Low-frequency slope** of magnitude plot: **-20n dB/dec** where n is the number of integrators (poles at origin) in G(s)H(s).
- **Phase plot starts at** -90°n (for n integrators) at low frequencies and **ends at** -90°(n+m) at high frequencies, where m is the number of finite poles.
- **Remember the two special Routh cases** — they are favorite KTU questions worth 7+ marks each.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
