# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Internal Model Control (IMC) Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ref1[R sub r] --> sum1((+))
    sum1 --> ctrlQ[Q sub IMC Controller]
    ctrlQ --> valve[Actuator u sub t]
    valve --> procGp[Process G sub p s]
    procGp --> outY[Output y sub t]
    outY --> outTerm[Plant Output]
    outY --> sum2((+))
    dist[d Disturbance] --> sum2
    sum2 --> errF[Feedback Error e sub f]
    errF --> sum1
    procGp2[Internal Model G sub p tilde] --> sum3((+))
    ctrlQ --> procGp2
    sum3 --> sum1
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The controller $Q$ acts on the *filtered* error (true error minus model-predicted response), making the loop **open-loop stable** if the model is perfect — the famous **perfect-control property** of IMC.

---

## 4.2 Model Predictive Control (MPC) — Receding Horizon

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Present
        measY[Measured y sub k] --> stateEst[State Estimator]
        stateEst --> optBlock
    end
    subgraph PredictHorizon
        optBlock[Optimizer: minimize J] --> seqU[u sub k, u sub k+1, ..., u sub k+M-1]
        seqU --> predY[Predicted y sub k+1 ... y sub k+P]
    end
    seqU -- "Apply only first move u sub k" --> plantMPC[Plant]
    plantMPC --> measY
    predY -.-> optBlock
    optBlock -.-> cons[Constraints: u min, u max, delta u max, y limits]
```

> **Receding Horizon Logic:** The optimizer computes $M$ future moves, but **only the first** is applied. At the next sampling instant, the horizon shifts forward by one step and the optimization is repeated with fresh measurements.

---

## 4.3 Fuzzy Logic PID — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    errSig[Error e sub t] --> fuzzIn1[Fuzzifier: e, de]
    derrSig[Change in Error de sub t] --> fuzzIn1
    fuzzIn1 --> infEng[Inference Engine: 49 rules]
    infEng --> outFuzzy[Fuzzy Outputs: Delta Kc, Delta tauI, Delta tauD]
    outFuzzy --> defuzz[Defuzzifier: Centroid]
    defuzz --> pidUpdate[PID Parameter Update]
    pidUpdate --> pidBlock[PID Controller G sub c sub s]
    pidBlock --> finalU[Control Signal u sub t]
```

> **Standard rule base:** 7 linguistic labels × 7 labels × 3 outputs (49 + 49 + 49 = 147 rules) — a 2-D lookup table that encodes operator heuristics.

---

## 4.4 Adaptive PID — Gain Scheduling Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sched[Scheduling Variable z] --> table[Lookup Table: Kc tauI tauD versus z]
    table --> pidAdapt[Adaptive PID G sub c sub s, t]
    refSig[Reference r] --> errSum((+))
    errSum --> pidAdapt
    pidAdapt --> uOut[Control u]
    uOut --> plantG[Plant G sub p]
    plantG --> yOut[Output y]
    yOut --> fb[Feedback]
    fb --> errSum
    plantG -.-> sched
```

> **Gain scheduling logic:** The auxiliary variable $z$ (e.g., throughput, conversion) is measured; a pre-stored table returns the *correct* PID parameters for that operating point; the loop continues with the new gains.

---

## 4.5 Master Comparison — All Four Methods in One Picture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classical[Classical Tuning: ZN, Cohen-Coon] --> modern
    modern[Modern Tuning] --> imc[IMC: model-based, 1-knob]
    modern --> mpc[MPC: optimize over horizon]
    modern --> fuzzy[Fuzzy PID: linguistic rules]
    modern --> adapt[Adaptive PID: online retuning]
    imc -.-> industry1[Distillation, bioreactors]
    mpc -.-> industry2[Refineries, FCCU]
    fuzzy -.-> industry3[HVAC, consumer goods]
    adapt -.-> industry4[Aerospace, robotics]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When asked to "compare" or "discuss" any modern method, students often write *only definitions* and lose 5–6 marks. You MUST include: **(i) block diagram / structure, (ii) tuning procedure or working principle, (iii) ONE real industrial application, (iv) ONE advantage + ONE limitation.** This is the difference between 7 marks and 14 marks.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Internal Model Control (IMC) and state the role of the filter parameter $\lambda$.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **IMC** is a model-based controller design philosophy in which an explicit process model $\tilde{G}_p(s)$ is embedded in the feedback loop, and the controller $Q(s)$ is designed as the inverse of the invertible part of the model multiplied by a low-pass filter $F(s) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda s + 1}$.
>
> **Role of $\lambda$:** $\lambda$ is the **closed-loop time constant / IMC filter**. It is the **single tuning parameter** that trades off **performance** (small $\lambda$ → fast, aggressive) against **robustness** (large $\lambda$ → slow, smooth, tolerant of model error). A practical lower bound is $\lambda \geq 0.8\,\theta$ to ensure robustness.
>
> *(1 mark definition + 1 mark filter form + 1 mark role of $\lambda$ — full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2: List any three advantages of Model Predictive Control (MPC) over classical PID.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Constraint handling:** MPC explicitly accounts for input (valve saturation), output (safety limits), and rate ($\Delta u$) constraints during optimization, whereas PID ignores them. *(1 mark)*
2. **Multi-variable control:** MPC natively handles MIMO (Multiple-Input Multiple-Output) systems with coupling, while PID requires multiple SISO loops with decouplers. *(1 mark)*
3. **Predictive horizon:** MPC uses a process model to look $P$ steps ahead, anticipating problems before they occur (e.g., a constraint violation in 5 minutes), giving earlier corrective action. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Question A and Question B below are alternative options. You answer ONE. Each carries 14 marks split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### 📌 QUESTION A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Derive the IMC-PID tuning relations for a First-Order Plus Dead Time (FOPDT) process. State clearly the role of the IMC filter $\lambda$ and the heuristic used to choose it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Process Model and Factorization** *(1 mark)*
$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1} = G_p^+(s) \cdot G_p^-(s)$$
with $G_p^+(s) = e^{-\theta s}$ (non-invertible) and $G_p^-(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau s + 1}$ (invertible).

**2. Design of $Q(s)$** *(1 mark)*
$$Q(s) = \frac{1}{G_p^-(s)} \cdot F(s) = \frac{\tau s + 1}{K_p(\lambda s + 1)}$$

**3. Conversion to Classical $G_c(s)$** *(1 mark)*
$$G_c(s) = \frac{Q(s)}{1 - G_p(s) Q(s)} = \frac{\tau s + 1}{K_p(\lambda s + 1 - e^{-\theta s})}$$

**4. Padé Approximation and Matching** *(2 marks)*
Applying the $(1,1)$ Padé $e^{-\theta s} \approx (1 - 0.5\theta s)/(1 + 0.5\theta s)$ and matching to the ideal PID form $G_{PID}(s) = \dfrac{K_c}{\tau_I s}(\tau_I \tau_D s^2 + \tau_I s + 1)$ yields:
$$K_c = \frac{\tau + 0.5\theta}{K_p(\lambda + 0.5\theta)}, \qquad \tau_I = \tau + 0.5\theta, \qquad \tau_D = \frac{\tau \theta}{2\tau + \theta}$$

**5. Role and Heuristic for $\lambda$** *(2 marks)*
- $\lambda$ is the **IMC filter time constant**; it dictates the **closed-loop speed of response**.
- **Trade-off:** small $\lambda$ → fast but sensitive to model error; large $\lambda$ → slow but robust.
- **Heuristic:** $\lambda = \max(0.1\tau,\ 0.8\theta,\ 0.1 \cdot t_{settle}/3)$.
- **Lower bound:** $\lambda \geq 0.8\theta$ for robustness.

---

#### (b) A jacketed CSTR has the FOPDT model $G_p(s) = \dfrac{3.0\,e^{-4s}}{10s + 1}$ (gain in °C per % valve, time in min). Design an IMC-PID controller using $\lambda = 0.8\theta$. Compare the resulting controller gain $K_c$ and integral time $\tau_I$ with the Ziegler–Nichols reaction-curve values for the same process. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Process parameters:**
$$K_p = 3.0,\ \tau = 10 \text{ min},\ \theta = 4 \text{ min}$$

**Step 2 — Choose $\lambda$:**
$$\lambda = 0.8 \times 4 = 3.2 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3 — IMC-PID values:** *(2 marks)*
$$K_c^{IMC} = \frac{\tau + 0.5\theta}{K_p(\lambda + 0.5\theta)} = \frac{10 + 2}{3.0 \times (3.2 + 2)} = \frac{12}{3.0 \times 5.2} = \frac{12}{15.6} = 0.769$$
$$\tau_I^{IMC} = \tau + 0.5\theta = 12 \text{ min}$$
$$\tau_D^{IMC} = \frac{\tau \theta}{2\tau + \theta} = \frac{10 \times 4}{20 + 4} = \frac{40}{24} = 1.667 \text{ min}$$

**Step 4 — Z-N Reaction Curve values:** *(2 marks)*

The reaction-curve method formulas:
$$K_c^{Z-N} = \frac{0.607\,\tau}{K_p\,L} = \frac{0.607 \times 10}{3.0 \times 4} = 0.506$$
$$\tau_I^{Z-N} = 1.4\,\tau = 14 \text{ min}$$
$$\tau_D^{Z-N} = 0.47\,\tau = 4.7 \text{ min}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison:** *(1 mark)*

| Parameter | IMC-PID | Z-N Reaction Curve | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| $K_c$ | 0.769 | 0.506 | IMC uses higher gain → faster |
| $\tau_I$ | 12 min | 14 min | IMC uses shorter $\tau_I$ → faster reset |
| $\tau_D$ | 1.667 min | 4.7 min | IMC uses smaller $\tau_D$ → less derivative noise amplification |
| Expected overshoot | < 5% | ~25% | IMC is **less aggressive** in overall character |

**Step 6 — Conclusion:** *(1 mark)*
Although the IMC gain is numerically higher, its derivative action is much smaller, producing a **smoother, more robust** response than Z-N. The IMC method requires the user to choose only **one** parameter ($\lambda$), making it operationally simpler than Z-N's three-parameter table.

### 📌 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Model Question Paper]`

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of Model Predictive Control (MPC). Define the prediction horizon $P$ and control horizon $M$, and explain the **receding horizon** concept. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition:** *(1 mark)*
MPC is a control strategy that uses an explicit dynamic model of the process to **predict** future outputs over a finite horizon, and then solves a **constrained optimization** problem at each sampling instant to compute the optimal control moves.

**2. Block Diagram (textual):** *(2 marks)*

```
r(k) ─→ (+) ─→ [ Optimizer: min J ] ──→ u(k) [applied]
            ↑           ↑
            |           | (uses internal model)
            |      [ Predictor: ŷ(k+1) ... ŷ(k+P) ]
   y(k) ─── |  ←───────────────────────────  
            |            Constraints: u_min, u_max, Δu_max
        [Plant Gp]
```

**3. Prediction and Control Horizons:** *(2 marks)*
- **Prediction Horizon $P$:** Number of future time steps over which the process output is forecasted. Larger $P$ → better anticipation but heavier computation.
- **Control Horizon $M$:** Number of future control moves the optimizer is allowed to vary (with $M \leq P$). After move $M$, the control signal is held constant.

**4. Receding Horizon Concept:** *(2 marks)*
At instant $k$, the optimizer solves for $\{u(k), u(k+1), \ldots, u(k+M-1)\}$. **Only $u(k)$ is applied** to the plant. At instant $k+1$, the horizon is shifted by one step: the optimizer now computes $\{u(k+1), u(k+2), \ldots, u(k+M)\}$, again keeping only the first move. This **rolls forward** at every step, hence the name.

**5. Why it works:** The optimizer's cost $J = \sum_{i=1}^{P} w_i (r_{k+i} - \hat y_{k+i})^2 + \sum_{j=1}^{M} \rho_j \Delta u_{k+j-1}^2$ penalises both tracking error and control effort, with constraints.

---

#### (b) Explain the architecture of a Fuzzy Logic PID controller with a block diagram. State the role of the fuzzifier, inference engine, and defuzzifier. Give one typical IF–THEN rule used to adjust $K_c$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Architecture Overview:** *(1 mark)*
A Fuzzy-PID uses the **error $e(t)$** and **change in error $\dot e(t)$** as crisp inputs. These are *fuzzified* into linguistic grades, processed by a rule base, *defuzzified* into a crisp output that adjusts the PID gains $K_c$, $\tau_I$, $\tau_D$.

**2. Block Diagram (textual):** *(2 marks)*
```
e(t), ė(t) ──→ [Fuzzifier] ──→ [Inference Engine + Rule Base] ──→ [Defuzzifier] ──→ ΔKc, ΔτI, ΔτD
                                                                                       │
                                                                          ┌────────────┘
                                                                          ▼
                                                                    [PID Controller]
                                                                          │
                                                                          ▼
                                                                       u(t)
```

**3. Role of Each Block:** *(2 marks)*

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **Fuzzifier** | Converts crisp numeric values of $e$ and $\dot e$ into membership grades in linguistic sets (NB, NM, NS, Z, PS, PM, PB). E.g., $e = 0.72$ → $\mu_{PB}(e) = 0.4$, $\mu_{PM}(e) = 0.6$. |
| **Inference Engine + Rule Base** | Applies IF–THEN rules that map the linguistic state of $(e, \dot e)$ to a linguistic recommendation for $\Delta K_c, \Delta \tau_I, \Delta \tau_D$. Uses Mamdani or Takagi–Sugeno inference. |
| **Defuzzifier** | Converts the fuzzy output (combined membership distribution) back to a crisp number — typically using the **centroid (centre of gravity)** formula. |

**4. Typical IF–THEN Rule:** *(1 mark)*

> **Rule:** *IF* error $e$ is **Positive Big (PB)** *AND* change in error $\dot e$ is **Zero (Z)**, *THEN* $\Delta K_c$ is **Positive Big (PB)**.
>
> *Interpretation:* When the controlled variable is far from setpoint and is not yet moving toward it, increase the controller gain aggressively.

**5. Application:** *(1 mark)*
Fuzzy PID is widely used in **pH control**, **HVAC systems**, and **induction motor drives** where the plant dynamics are nonlinear and human-experience-based rules outperform fixed-gain PID.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Month KTU Revision — 60-Second Summary**

- ✅ **IMC definition** = Model-based controller with $Q(s) = [G_p^-]^{-1} F(s)$ and $F(s) = 1/(\lambda s + 1)$.
- ✅ **IMC-PID for FOPDT:** $K_c = (\tau + 0.5\theta) / [K_p(\lambda + 0.5\theta)]$, $\tau_I = \tau + 0.5\theta$, $\tau_D = \tau\theta/(2\tau + \theta)$.
- ✅ **Filter rule:** $\lambda \geq 0.8\theta$ (robustness lower bound).
- ✅ **IMC is single-knob tuning** — only $\lambda$ trades off speed vs robustness.
- ✅ **MPC = Predict + Optimize over horizon + Apply only first move** (Receding Horizon).
- ✅ **MPC key advantage:** explicit handling of input, output, and rate constraints.
- ✅ **Prediction horizon $P$** = how far we look ahead; **Control horizon $M$** = how many moves we compute.
- ✅ **Fuzzy-PID inputs:** $e(t)$ and $\dot e(t)$; **output:** adjustment to $K_c, \tau_I, \tau_D$.
- ✅ **Fuzzy-PID components:** Fuzzifier → Inference Engine (with rule base) → Defuzzifier (centroid).
- ✅ **Adaptive PID types:** Gain Scheduling, Auto-Tuner (relay feedback), MRAC.
- ✅ **MRAC** uses a reference model + Lyapunov-based update law to track desired closed-loop behaviour.
- ✅ **Gain Scheduling** is the simplest adaptive scheme — uses a lookup table indexed by an auxiliary variable.
- ✅ **IMC is more robust** than Z-N; **MPC is more powerful** but computationally heavy; **Fuzzy is heuristic-based**; **Adaptive is self-correcting**.
- ✅ **Industrial mapping to remember for 3-markers:** Refineries → MPC, pH/HVAC → Fuzzy, Distillation → IMC-PID, Aerospace → MRAC.
